questions.q: What is Python and what are its key features?
questions.short: Python is a high-level, interpreted, dynamically typed, general-purpose language known for its readable syntax and large ecosystem.
questions.detailed:
Python was created by Guido van Rossum in 1991. Its key features:
  • Interpreted: code executes line by line, no compilation step needed.
  • Dynamically typed: variable types are checked at runtime.
  • High-level: abstracts memory management and hardware details.
  • General-purpose: used for web, data science, automation, AI/ML.
  • Batteries-included: large standard library out of the box.
  • Readable syntax: enforced indentation keeps code clean.
Key Point

Python does not require type declarations. The interpreter infers types at runtime.
followups: What are Python's limitations?
How is Python different from Java?
What is duck typing?

questions.q: Why is Python widely used in Data Analytics and Data Science?
questions.short: Python dominates data fields because of its simple syntax, powerful libraries (Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn), and strong community.
questions.detailed:
Python is the go-to language for data work because:
  • Pandas and NumPy provide fast, expressive data manipulation.
  • Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly handle visualisation.
  • Scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch power machine learning.
  • Low learning curve: data scientists often come from non-CS backgrounds.
  • Jupyter Notebooks enable interactive, exploratory analysis.
  • Huge community with constant library updates.
Pro Tip

Mention specific libraries relevant to the role you are interviewing for.
followups: What Python libraries have you used for data analysis?
How does Python compare to R for data science?

questions.q: What is PEP 8 and why does it matter?
questions.short: PEP 8 is Python's official style guide covering indentation, naming, spacing, and code layout to keep code consistent and readable.
questions.detailed:
PEP stands for Python Enhancement Proposal. PEP 8 defines:
  • 4 spaces per indentation level (never tabs).
  • Maximum 79 characters per line.
  • snake_case for variables and functions; PascalCase for classes; UPPER_CASE for constants.
  • 2 blank lines before top-level functions/classes; 1 blank line inside classes.
  • Imports on separate lines: standard library first, then third-party, then local.
Key Point

Tools like flake8, pylint, and black can auto-check or auto-format your code against PEP 8.
followups: What tools enforce PEP 8?
What is PEP 20 (Zen of Python)?
What is a linter?

questions.q: Differentiate between interpreted and compiled languages.
questions.short: Interpreted languages execute code line by line at runtime. Compiled languages translate the entire source to machine code before execution.
questions.detailed:
Interpreted (Python, JavaScript, Ruby):
  • Source code is read and executed directly by an interpreter.
  • No separate compilation step — faster development and debugging.
  • Errors caught at runtime when that line is reached.
  • Usually slower execution than compiled languages.
Compiled (C, C++, Rust):
  • Source code is translated to machine code before execution.
  • Faster runtime since translation is done upfront.
  • Errors caught at compile time.
Key Point

Python is technically compiled to bytecode (.pyc) first, then interpreted by the Python Virtual Machine (PVM). So it sits between the two categories.

Common Trap

Do not say Python is purely interpreted. Mention the bytecode step — it shows deeper understanding.
followups: What is bytecode in Python?
What is the Python Virtual Machine?
What are .pyc files?

questions.q: Why is Python called a dynamically typed language?
questions.short: Python is dynamically typed because variable types are determined at runtime based on the value assigned, not declared in advance.
questions.detailed:
In a dynamically typed language, you do not declare the type:
  • x = 10 makes x an int.
  • x = 'hello' reassigns x as a str — perfectly valid.
  • The same variable can hold different types at different points.
This contrasts with statically typed languages like Java where you must write int x = 10; and cannot change the type.
Key Point

Dynamic typing makes Python fast to write, but type errors only appear at runtime.

Common Trap

Dynamic typing does NOT mean weakly typed. Python is strongly typed — it does not silently coerce types like JavaScript does. '5' + 5 raises a TypeError in Python.
followups: What is the difference between dynamic and weak typing?
What are type hints in Python?
What is type coercion?

questions.q: Differentiate between high-level and low-level programming languages.
questions.short: High-level languages (Python, Java) abstract away hardware. Low-level languages (Assembly, C) are closer to machine instructions.
questions.detailed:
High-level languages:
  • Abstract memory management, CPU registers, and hardware details.
  • Easier to read, write, and maintain.
  • Examples: Python, Java, JavaScript, Ruby.
Low-level languages:
  • Closer to binary and machine instructions.
  • Give the programmer direct control over memory and hardware.
  • Harder to write but faster to execute.
  • Examples: Assembly, C, C++.
Key Point

Python is high-level. You never manually allocate or free memory — the garbage collector handles it.
followups: What is garbage collection?
Is C a high-level or low-level language?
What is abstraction in programming?

questions.q: What are comments in Python? Explain the different types.
questions.short: Comments are non-executable lines that explain code. Python has single-line comments (#) and docstrings (triple quotes used for documentation).
questions.detailed:
Python supports two comment styles:
  • Single-line: Start with #. Everything after # on that line is ignored by the interpreter.
  • Docstrings: Triple quotes """...""" placed immediately after a function, class, or module definition. These are not true comments — they are string literals stored as __doc__.
Key Point

Access a function's docstring with help(fn) or fn.__doc__.

Pro Tip

Use comments to explain WHY, not WHAT. The code itself should show what is happening.
followups: What is a docstring?
How do you access a function's docstring?
What is the difference between a comment and a docstring?

questions.q: What is indentation in Python and what issues arise from incorrect indentation?
questions.short: Indentation in Python defines code blocks. The standard is 4 spaces per level. Incorrect indentation causes an IndentationError.
questions.detailed:
Python uses indentation instead of curly braces to define blocks:
  • Every block (if, for, while, def, class) must be indented consistently.
  • The standard is 4 spaces per level (PEP 8 requirement).
  • Never mix tabs and spaces — Python 3 raises a TabError.
  • IndentationError is raised if indentation is inconsistent.
  • IndentationError: unexpected indent occurs when a line is indented without starting a new block.
Key Point

Indentation is part of Python syntax, not just style. Unlike C or Java, incorrect indentation breaks the program.

Common Trap

Configure your editor to convert tabs to 4 spaces automatically. Mixed indentation is invisible to the eye but breaks Python 3.
followups: What error does wrong indentation cause?
How many spaces does PEP 8 recommend?
Can you use tabs in Python?

questions.q: What are Python keywords and why are they important?
questions.short: Keywords are reserved words with special meaning in Python (if, else, for, while, return, class). They cannot be used as variable names.
questions.detailed:
Python has 35 keywords (Python 3.12). Key categories:
  • Control flow: if, elif, else, for, while, break, continue, pass
  • Functions/Classes: def, class, return, yield, lambda
  • Logical: and, or, not, in, is
  • Scope: global, nonlocal
  • Exceptions: try, except, finally, raise, with, as
  • Values: True, False, None
  • Imports: import, from, as
Key Point

View all keywords with: import keyword; print(keyword.kwlist).

Common Trap

True and False are keywords and must be capitalised. true is not a keyword — it is just an identifier.
followups: How many keywords does Python have?
Can you use keywords as variable names?
What is the difference between a keyword and an identifier?

questions.q: What are identifiers and what are the naming rules in Python?
questions.short: Identifiers are names given to variables, functions, classes, and other objects. They must start with a letter or underscore and are case-sensitive.
questions.detailed:
Rules for valid identifiers:
  • Must start with a letter (a-z, A-Z) or underscore (_).
  • Can contain letters, digits (0-9), and underscores.
  • Cannot start with a digit.
  • Cannot be a keyword.
  • Case-sensitive: name, Name, and NAME are three different identifiers.
Naming conventions (PEP 8):
  • snake_case for variables and functions.
  • PascalCase for classes.
  • UPPER_CASE for constants.
  • _single_underscore prefix for internal/private use.
  • __double_underscore prefix triggers name mangling in classes.
Common Trap

Starting an identifier with double underscore (__name) inside a class triggers name mangling. This is not the same as making it private.
followups: What is name mangling?
What does a single underscore prefix mean?
What is the difference between a keyword and an identifier?

questions.q: What are variables and how does variable assignment work in Python?
questions.short: A variable is a named reference to an object in memory. Assignment with = binds a name to an object — not a copy of the value.
questions.detailed:
In Python, variables are labels (references) that point to objects in memory, not containers:
  • x = 10 creates an integer object 10 and binds the name x to it.
  • y = x makes y point to the SAME object as x.
  • Python uses reference semantics: multiple names can point to one object.
Key Point

Use id(x) to see the memory address an object lives at. Two variables with the same id() point to the same object.

Common Trap

For mutable objects like lists, y = x creates an alias — not a copy. Modifying y also modifies x because they point to the same list. Use y = x.copy() to get a true copy.
followups: What is the difference between a variable and an object?
What is id() in Python?
What is the difference between shallow and deep copy?

questions.q: Differentiate between local and global variables.
questions.short: Local variables exist only inside a function. Global variables are defined at module level and accessible from anywhere in the module.
questions.detailed:
• Local variable: Created inside a function. Destroyed when the function returns. Only accessible within that function.
  • Global variable: Created at the top level of a module. Accessible from anywhere in that module.
Example: a function that assigns x = 'local' creates a new local variable that shadows the global x, leaving the global unchanged.
Key Point

To modify a global variable inside a function, declare it with global x first.

Common Trap

If Python sees any assignment to a name inside a function, it treats that name as local throughout the entire function — even before the assignment line. Reading it before assignment causes UnboundLocalError.
followups: What is the LEGB rule?
What is UnboundLocalError?
When should you avoid global variables?

questions.q: Explain the LEGB rule for variable scope in Python.
questions.short: LEGB stands for Local, Enclosing, Global, Built-in — the order Python searches for a variable name.
questions.detailed:
When Python resolves a name, it searches these scopes in order and stops at the first match:
  • L - Local: Inside the current function.
  • E - Enclosing: Inside any outer functions (for closures/nested functions).
  • G - Global: At the module (file) level.
  • B - Built-in: Python's built-in namespace (len, print, range, etc.).
If the name is not found in any scope, Python raises NameError.
Key Point

LEGB only applies to name lookup, not assignment. Assignment always creates a local variable unless you explicitly use global or nonlocal.

Pro Tip

You can accidentally shadow built-ins by naming a variable list or len. Avoid this.
followups: What is the nonlocal keyword?
What is the global keyword?
What is a closure?
What is NameError?

questions.q: What is a namespace in Python?
questions.short: A namespace is a mapping of names to objects. Python maintains separate namespaces for built-ins, globals, and each function call.
questions.detailed:
A namespace is internally a dictionary mapping identifiers to objects:
  • Built-in namespace: Contains print, len, range, etc. Always available.
  • Global namespace: Created when a module loads. Contains all top-level names.
  • Local namespace: Created on each function call. Destroyed when the function returns.
  • Enclosing namespace: Exists in nested functions between local and global.
Key Point

Namespace and scope are related but different. Scope is the region of code where a namespace is accessible. Namespace is the actual storage mapping.

Pro Tip

Inspect the current namespace with dir() or the global namespace with globals().
followups: What is the difference between namespace and scope?
What does dir() return?
What is the LEGB rule?

questions.q: What is variable shadowing?
questions.short: Variable shadowing occurs when a variable in an inner scope has the same name as one in an outer scope, hiding the outer one within that scope.
questions.detailed:
When a local variable name matches a global or built-in name, the inner one shadows (hides) the outer:
  • Inside the function, the name refers to the local variable, not the global.
  • The outer variable is completely unchanged.
  • You can even shadow built-ins like list, len, print — this is dangerous.
Common Trap

Writing list = [1, 2, 3] shadows the built-in list type. After this, list() no longer works in that scope. Use descriptive names to avoid this.

Key Point

Python never warns about shadowing. Use a linter like flake8 with the shadow-builtins plugin to catch accidental shadowing.
followups: How do you access the shadowed global variable?
What built-ins are commonly accidentally shadowed?
What is variable scope?

questions.q: What is the nonlocal keyword and when is it used?
questions.short: The nonlocal keyword lets an inner (nested) function read and modify a variable from its enclosing outer function's scope.
questions.detailed:
Without nonlocal, an inner function can read enclosing scope variables but cannot rebind them:
  • nonlocal x tells Python to look in the enclosing scope for x rather than creating a new local.
  • Only works in nested functions — it cannot reach the global scope.
  • Useful in closures when the inner function needs to maintain state (e.g. a counter).
Key Point

Use global to modify a module-level variable. Use nonlocal to modify a variable from an enclosing function. They serve different scopes.

Common Trap

If you omit nonlocal, the assignment inside the inner function silently creates a new local variable and the enclosing variable remains unchanged.
followups: What is a closure?
What is the difference between nonlocal and global?
What is the LEGB rule?

questions.q: What is the global keyword and when is it used?
questions.short: The global keyword declares that a variable inside a function refers to the module-level global, not a new local variable.
questions.detailed:
By default, any assignment inside a function creates a local variable. global overrides this:
  • Declare global x at the top of the function.
  • Any read or write to x then affects the global x.
  • Multiple globals can be declared on one line: global x, y, z.
Key Point

You do NOT need global just to read a global variable. You only need it if you want to reassign it inside a function.

Common Trap

Overusing global variables makes code hard to test and debug. Prefer passing values as function parameters and returning results.
followups: When should you avoid global variables?
What is the nonlocal keyword?
What is the LEGB rule?

questions.q: What is object identity in Python?
questions.short: Object identity is a unique ID assigned to every object in memory. Use id() to retrieve it, and the is operator to compare it.
questions.detailed:
Every Python object has three properties: identity (id), type, and value:
  • id(obj) returns a unique integer representing the memory address of the object.
  • The is operator checks if two names point to the SAME object (same id).
  • The == operator checks if two objects have the SAME VALUE.
Key Point

CPython caches small integers (-5 to 256) and interned strings, so a is b may be True for small ints even when assigned separately. Do not rely on is for value comparison.

Common Trap

Never write if x == None. Always write if x is None. None is a singleton — there is only one None object in memory — so identity comparison is correct and faster.
followups: What is the difference between is and ==?
What is integer caching in CPython?
What is a singleton?

questions.q: Differentiate between mutable and immutable data types in Python.
questions.short: Mutable objects can be changed after creation. Immutable objects cannot — any modification creates a new object.
questions.detailed:
Mutable (can be changed in-place):
  • list: append, remove, sort, etc. all modify the original.
  • dict: add/remove/update keys.
  • set: add/discard elements.
Immutable (cannot be changed):
  • int, float, bool: any arithmetic creates a new object.
  • str: every string operation returns a new string.
  • tuple: elements cannot be added, removed, or changed.
  • frozenset: immutable version of set.
Key Point

Immutable objects are hashable and can be used as dictionary keys or set elements. Mutable objects cannot.

Common Trap

Immutable does not mean the variable cannot be reassigned. x = 5; x = 10 is valid — you are rebinding the name x to a new object, not changing the object itself.
followups: What is hashability?
Why are tuple keys allowed in dicts but not lists?
What is the difference between rebinding and mutation?

questions.q: Differentiate between primitive and non-primitive data types in Python.
questions.short: Primitive types represent single simple values (int, float, bool, str). Non-primitive types are collections or complex structures (list, dict, tuple, set).
questions.detailed:
Primitive data types:
  • int: whole numbers. e.g. 42, -10.
  • float: decimal numbers. e.g. 3.14.
  • bool: True or False. Subclass of int.
  • str: sequence of characters. e.g. "hello".
Non-primitive data types:
  • list: ordered, mutable collection.
  • tuple: ordered, immutable collection.
  • set: unordered, unique elements.
  • dict: key-value pairs.
  • NoneType: the type of None.
Key Point

In Python, even primitive types are objects. isinstance(True, int) returns True because bool is a subclass of int.
followups: What is the type of True in Python?
Is None a primitive type?
What is NoneType?

questions.q: List all built-in data types available in Python.
questions.short: Python's built-in data types include int, float, complex, bool, str, list, tuple, set, frozenset, dict, and NoneType.
questions.detailed:
Python organises built-in types into categories:
  • Numeric: int, float, complex, bool
  • Sequence: str, list, tuple, range, bytes
  • Mapping: dict
  • Set: set, frozenset
  • Boolean: bool (subclass of int)
  • None: NoneType (the type of None)
Key Point

Use type(x) to check the type of any object, or isinstance(x, int) to check including subclasses.

Pro Tip

bool is a subclass of int. So True + True equals 2 and True == 1 is True. This is a common interview trick question.
followups: What is the type of True?
What is the difference between type() and isinstance()?
What is NoneType?

questions.q: What is type casting? Differentiate between explicit and implicit type casting.
questions.short: Type casting converts a value from one type to another. Explicit casting uses int(), str(), float(). Implicit casting is done automatically by Python.
questions.detailed:
Explicit type casting (you do it manually):
  • int("42") converts string "42" to integer 42.
  • str(3.14) converts float to string "3.14".
  • float("3.14") converts string to float.
  • list((1, 2, 3)) converts tuple to list.
  • bool(0) returns False; bool(1) returns True.
Implicit type casting (Python does it automatically):
  • Python widens types automatically in arithmetic: 3 + 2.0 gives 5.0 (int promoted to float).
  • Python does NOT implicitly convert between str and int like JavaScript does.
Key Point

Python is strongly typed. It will not silently convert "5" + 5 — this raises a TypeError. You must explicitly cast: int("5") + 5.

Common Trap

int(3.9) gives 3, not 4. It truncates, not rounds. Use round() if you want rounding.
followups: What is the difference between int() and round()?
Does Python do implicit type conversion?
What is type coercion?

questions.q: What is hashability and which types are hashable?
questions.short: A hashable object has a fixed hash value throughout its lifetime. Only hashable (immutable) objects can be used as dictionary keys or set elements.
questions.detailed:
Python uses hash values to quickly locate objects in dictionaries and sets:
  • An object is hashable if it implements __hash__() and __eq__().
  • All immutable types are hashable: int, str, tuple (if elements are also hashable), frozenset.
  • Mutable types are not hashable: list, dict, set.
Key Point

If you try to use a list as a dict key, Python raises TypeError: unhashable type: "list". Use a tuple instead.

Common Trap

A tuple containing a list is NOT hashable, even though tuples are normally hashable. The hashability of a tuple depends on the hashability of all its elements.
followups: Why can't lists be used as dictionary keys?
What is the hash() function?
What is a frozenset?

questions.q: Differentiate between type() and isinstance().
questions.short: type() returns the exact type of an object. isinstance() checks if an object is an instance of a class or any of its subclasses.
questions.detailed:
• type(x) returns the exact class. It does not consider inheritance.
  • isinstance(x, cls) returns True if x is an instance of cls or any subclass of cls.
  • isinstance(x, (int, str)) — you can pass a tuple of types to check against multiple.
Example: type(True) == bool is True, but type(True) == int is False, even though bool is a subclass of int. Meanwhile, isinstance(True, int) is True.
Key Point

Prefer isinstance() over type() == in most cases. It correctly handles subclasses and is the Pythonic approach.

Common Trap

Using type(x) == int to check types will fail for subclasses. isinstance(True, int) is True because bool subclasses int, but type(True) == int is False.
followups: What is the difference between type() and isinstance()?
What does subclass mean?
Is bool a subclass of int in Python?

questions.q: What is NoneType in Python?
questions.short: NoneType is the type of the None object. None represents the absence of a value. It is a singleton — only one None exists in memory.
questions.detailed:
• None is the Python equivalent of null in other languages.
  • Its type is NoneType: type(None) returns .
  • None is a singleton — there is exactly one None object in the entire Python process.
  • Functions without a return statement implicitly return None.
  • None is falsy: bool(None) returns False.
Key Point

Always compare with None using is or is not, never with ==. Since it is a singleton, identity comparison is more correct and faster.

Common Trap

if x: and if x is not None: are NOT equivalent. 0, "", [] are all falsy but not None. Use is not None when you specifically want to check for None.
followups: What is a singleton?
How do you check if a variable is None?
What does a function return if it has no return statement?

questions.q: What is dynamic typing and strong typing in Python?
questions.short: Dynamic typing means variable types are checked at runtime. Strong typing means Python does not silently coerce types in operations.
questions.detailed:
Dynamic typing:
  • You do not declare variable types.
  • The interpreter assigns types based on values at runtime.
  • A variable can be reassigned to a different type at any point.
Strong typing:
  • Python enforces type rules in operations.
  • "5" + 5 raises TypeError — Python will not silently convert the string to an int.
  • Contrast with JavaScript (weakly typed): "5" + 5 gives "55" there.
Key Point

Python is both dynamically typed AND strongly typed. These are independent concepts. Dynamic = types checked at runtime. Strong = no implicit coercion.

Common Trap

Confusing dynamic typing with weak typing is a very common mistake. Python is dynamic but strong. JavaScript is dynamic and weak.
followups: What is the difference between dynamic and weak typing?
What is type coercion?
How does Python compare to JavaScript in typing?

questions.q: Compare lists and tuples with real-world use cases.
questions.short: Lists are mutable, ordered collections. Tuples are immutable, ordered collections. Use lists for data that changes, tuples for data that should not change.
questions.detailed:
• List: mutable, created with [], supports append/remove/sort. Slightly slower due to overhead of mutability.
  • Tuple: immutable, created with (), faster and memory-efficient, can be used as dict keys.
When to use each:
  • List: shopping cart items, rows of data, anything that grows or shrinks.
  • Tuple: RGB colour values, coordinates (x, y), database records, function return values with multiple parts.
  • Tuple: when you want to guarantee the data will not change.
Key Point

Tuples are slightly faster than lists for iteration and indexing because Python can optimise immutable structures.

Common Trap

A single-element tuple requires a trailing comma: (42,). Without the comma, (42) is just parentheses around an int, not a tuple.
followups: Can a tuple contain mutable objects?
How do you create a single-element tuple?
Why are tuples hashable but lists are not?

questions.q: Classify the different types of operators available in Python.
questions.short: Python operators are classified into: arithmetic, assignment, comparison, logical, identity, membership, bitwise, and the walrus operator.
questions.detailed:
• Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, //, %, **
  • Assignment: =, +=, -=, *=, //=, **=
  • Comparison: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=
  • Logical: and, or, not
  • Identity: is, is not
  • Membership: in, not in
  • Bitwise: &, |, ^, ~, <<, >>
  • Walrus (assignment expression, Python 3.8+): :=
Key Point

Comparison operators (==, >, etc.), logical operators (and, or, not), identity, and membership operators all return boolean values.
followups: What is the walrus operator?
What is operator overloading?
What is operator precedence?

questions.q: Differentiate between ==, = and the is operator in Python.
questions.short: = is assignment. == checks value equality. is checks object identity (same object in memory).
questions.detailed:
• =: Assignment operator. Binds a name to an object. x = 5.
  • ==: Equality operator. Compares values. Returns True if values are equal.
  • is: Identity operator. Returns True if both variables point to the exact same object in memory (same id()).
Example: a = [1,2]; b = [1,2] — a == b is True (same values), but a is b is False (different objects).
Key Point

For None, always use is: if x is None. None is a singleton, so identity check is correct.

Common Trap

Do not use is to compare integers or strings outside the cache range. CPython caches small integers (-5 to 256), so a is b might be True for small ints — but this is an implementation detail, not guaranteed.
followups: What is object identity?
What is integer caching in CPython?
When should you use is vs ==?

questions.q: Explain arithmetic operators in Python with examples.
questions.short: Arithmetic operators perform mathematical operations: + (add), - (subtract), * (multiply), / (divide), // (floor divide), % (modulus), ** (exponent).
questions.detailed:
• +: Addition. Also concatenates strings and lists.
  • -: Subtraction.
  • *: Multiplication. Also repeats strings/lists: "ab" * 3 gives "ababab".
  • /: True division. Always returns a float in Python 3: 7 / 2 gives 3.5.
  • //: Floor division. Returns the floor of the result: 7 // 2 gives 3.
  • %: Modulus. Returns the remainder: 7 % 2 gives 1.
  • **: Exponentiation: 2 ** 3 gives 8.
Key Point

In Python 2, / between two integers did integer division. In Python 3, / always returns a float. Use // for integer division.

Common Trap

** is right-associative: 2 ** 3 ** 2 evaluates as 2 ** (3 ** 2) = 2 ** 9 = 512, not (2 ** 3) ** 2 = 64.
followups: What is the difference between / and //
What is floor division?
What does the modulus operator return for negative numbers?

questions.q: Compare / (true division), // (floor division), and % (modulus) operators.
questions.short: / always returns a float (true division). // returns the floor of the result (floor division). % returns the remainder.
questions.detailed:
• / True division: 7 / 2 = 3.5, 6 / 2 = 3.0 (always float in Python 3).
  • // Floor division: 7 // 2 = 3. Rounds DOWN to nearest integer (toward negative infinity).
  • % Modulus: 7 % 2 = 1 (remainder after division).
Relationship: a == (a // b) * b + (a % b) always holds.
Key Point

Floor division rounds toward negative infinity, not toward zero. So -7 // 2 = -4, not -3.

Common Trap

-7 // 2 gives -4 (not -3) because floor division rounds DOWN. This surprises many developers expecting truncation toward zero.
followups: What is the modulus operator?
What is divmod()?
How does floor division handle negative numbers?

questions.q: Differentiate between the * and ** operators.
questions.short: In arithmetic, * is multiplication and ** is exponentiation. In function definitions, *args captures positional arguments and **kwargs captures keyword arguments.
questions.detailed:
In arithmetic:
  • *: Multiplication — 3 * 4 = 12.
  • **: Exponentiation — 2 ** 8 = 256.
  • * also repeats sequences — "ab" * 3 = "ababab".
In function signatures:
  • *args: Packs extra positional arguments into a tuple.
  • **kwargs: Packs extra keyword arguments into a dict.
In function calls (unpacking):
  • fn(*[1,2,3]): Unpacks list as positional arguments.
  • fn(**{"a":1}): Unpacks dict as keyword arguments.
Key Point

The names args and kwargs are conventions only. The * and ** are the actual syntax. You can use *nums or **options if you prefer.
followups: What is *args and **kwargs?
What is argument unpacking?
What is the difference between * and ** in function calls?

questions.q: Explain operator precedence in Python (PEMDAS/BODMAS).
questions.short: Python follows PEMDAS/BODMAS: Parentheses, Exponentiation, Multiplication/Division, Addition/Subtraction. Among same-level operators, left-to-right except ** which is right-to-left.
questions.detailed:
Full precedence order (highest to lowest):
  • (): Parentheses — always evaluated first.
  • **: Exponentiation — right-to-left associativity.
  • +x, -x, ~x: Unary operators.
  • *, /, //, %: Multiplication/Division — left-to-right.
  • +, -: Addition/Subtraction — left-to-right.
  • <<, >>: Bitwise shifts.
  • &, ^, |: Bitwise operators.
  • ==, !=, >, <, in, is: Comparisons.
  • not: Logical NOT.
  • and: Logical AND.
  • or: Logical OR.
Key Point

When in doubt, use parentheses. They make code more readable and remove any ambiguity about operator precedence.
followups: What is the associativity of **?
Does not have higher precedence than and?
What is short-circuit evaluation?

questions.q: What are membership operators (in, not in) and when should they be used?
questions.short: Membership operators (in, not in) test whether a value exists in a sequence or collection. They return True or False.
questions.detailed:
• x in seq: Returns True if x is found in seq.
  • x not in seq: Returns True if x is NOT found in seq.
  • Works with: strings, lists, tuples, sets, dicts (checks keys), and any iterable.
Performance varies by data type:
  • List/tuple: O(n) — scans each element.
  • Set/dict: O(1) average — uses hash lookup.
  • String: checks for substring.
Key Point

Use sets instead of lists when frequently checking membership. x in my_set is O(1) vs x in my_list which is O(n).
followups: What is the time complexity of in for a list vs set?
Can you use in with a generator?
What is the difference between in for strings and lists?

questions.q: Explain identity operators (is, is not) with examples.
questions.short: Identity operators (is, is not) check whether two variables point to the same object in memory, not whether their values are equal.
questions.detailed:
• a is b: Returns True if a and b are the exact same object (same id()).
  • a is not b: Returns True if they are different objects.
Common correct uses:
  • if x is None: Checking for None (None is a singleton).
  • if x is True or if x is False: Checking for the exact boolean (rarely needed).
  • Checking if two variables are the same container object (before and after a function call).
Key Point

Use == for value comparison. Use is only for singletons (None, True, False) or when you specifically need to check object identity.

Common Trap

Do not use is to compare numbers or strings in general. CPython's integer caching and string interning can make is return True unexpectedly, but this is an implementation detail not guaranteed across all Python versions.
followups: What is a singleton in Python?
What is integer caching?
What is string interning?

questions.q: What are assignment operators in Python?
questions.short: Assignment operators bind a value to a variable. Augmented assignment operators (+=, -=, *=, etc.) combine assignment with an operation.
questions.detailed:
• =: Simple assignment. x = 5.
  • +=: Add and assign. x += 3 means x = x + 3.
  • -=: Subtract and assign.
  • *=: Multiply and assign.
  • /=: Divide and assign (result is always float).
  • //=: Floor divide and assign.
  • %=: Modulus and assign.
  • **=: Exponent and assign.
  • :=: Walrus operator (Python 3.8+) — assigns and returns the value in one expression.
Key Point

For mutable types, += uses __iadd__ (in-place add), which modifies the object directly. For immutable types like int/str, += creates a new object and rebinds the name.

Common Trap

For lists, x += [1] modifies the list in-place. But x = x + [1] creates a new list. This matters when multiple variables reference the same list.
followups: What is the walrus operator?
What is the difference between += and = + for lists?
What is __iadd__?

questions.q: What are bitwise operators and when are they used?
questions.short: Bitwise operators work on the binary representation of integers: & (AND), | (OR), ^ (XOR), ~ (NOT), << (left shift), >> (right shift).
questions.detailed:
• &: Bitwise AND. Both bits must be 1. 5 & 3 = 1 (101 & 011 = 001).
  • |: Bitwise OR. Either bit is 1. 5 | 3 = 7 (101 | 011 = 111).
  • ^: Bitwise XOR. Bits differ. 5 ^ 3 = 6 (101 ^ 011 = 110).
  • ~: Bitwise NOT. Flips all bits. ~5 = -6.
  • <<: Left shift. 5 << 1 = 10 (multiply by 2).
  • >>: Right shift. 5 >> 1 = 2 (floor divide by 2).
Key Point

Left shift by n is equivalent to multiplying by 2^n. Right shift by n is equivalent to floor dividing by 2^n. Bitwise operations are very fast at the hardware level.

Pro Tip

Bitwise operators are commonly used for flags, permissions systems, and low-level data manipulation. In interviews, they appear in problems like "check if a number is a power of 2": n & (n-1) == 0.
followups: What is the result of ~5 in Python?
How do you check if a number is a power of 2 using bitwise operators?
When would you use bitwise operators in practice?

questions.q: What are logical operators in Python?
questions.short: Logical operators (and, or, not) combine boolean expressions. They use short-circuit evaluation and can return non-boolean values.
questions.detailed:
• and: Returns the first falsy value, or the last value if all are truthy. 5 and 0 = 0; 5 and 3 = 3.
  • or: Returns the first truthy value, or the last value if all are falsy. 0 or 5 = 5; 0 or False = False.
  • not: Returns the boolean inverse. not 0 = True; not 5 = False.
Short-circuit evaluation:
  • and stops and returns the first falsy value without evaluating the rest.
  • or stops and returns the first truthy value without evaluating the rest.
Key Point

and and or do NOT always return True/False — they return actual values. x = x or "default" is a common Python idiom for default values.

Common Trap

not always returns a boolean. But and and or return one of their operands, not necessarily a boolean.
followups: What is short-circuit evaluation?
What does and return if all values are truthy?
What is the common idiom using or for default values?

questions.q: What is operator overloading and how does it work in Python?
questions.short: Operator overloading lets you define how Python's built-in operators behave for your custom classes using dunder (magic) methods.
questions.detailed:
You define operator behaviour in a class by implementing special dunder methods:
  • __add__(self, other): defines +
  • __sub__(self, other): defines -
  • __mul__(self, other): defines *
  • __eq__(self, other): defines ==
  • __lt__(self, other): defines <
  • __len__(self): defines len()
  • __str__(self): defines str() and print()
Example: a Vector class can implement __add__ so v1 + v2 adds corresponding components.
Key Point

Python uses operator overloading extensively internally. + on strings calls str.__add__, on lists calls list.__add__.
followups: What are dunder methods?
How do you make a class printable?
What is the difference between __str__ and __repr__?

questions.q: What is the walrus operator (:=) in Python 3.8+?
questions.short: The walrus operator (:=) assigns a value to a variable and returns that value in the same expression, useful in while loops and comprehensions.
questions.detailed:
Introduced in Python 3.8 (PEP 572), the walrus operator allows assignment inside expressions:
  • Useful in while loops to avoid calling a function twice: while chunk := f.read(8192):
  • Useful in comprehensions to reuse a computed value: [y := f(x), y**2, y**3]
  • Useful in if statements: if m := re.match(pattern, s): print(m.group())
Key Point

The walrus operator reduces redundant computation and makes some patterns more concise. But overusing it reduces readability.

Common Trap

The walrus operator (:=) and the assignment operator (=) are different. := can be used inside expressions (conditions, comprehensions). = cannot.
followups: What Python version introduced the walrus operator?
What is PEP 572?
When should you use := vs =?

questions.q: Which operators in Python return a boolean value?
questions.short: Comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=), logical operators (and, or, not), identity operators (is, is not), and membership operators (in, not in) return boolean values.
questions.detailed:
• Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >= — always return True or False.
  • Logical NOT: not x — always returns True or False.
  • Logical AND/OR: and, or — return one of their operands, not necessarily a boolean (see short-circuit evaluation).
  • Identity: is, is not — always return True or False.
  • Membership: in, not in — always return True or False.
Key Point

and and or do not always return booleans — they return one of the operands using short-circuit logic. Only not is guaranteed to return True or False.

Common Trap

bool(x and y) forces a boolean. But x and y itself returns x if x is falsy, otherwise y. This is a common source of confusion.
followups: What is short-circuit evaluation?
What does or return when both operands are truthy?
What is truthiness in Python?

questions.q: Are strings mutable in Python? Explain.
questions.short: Strings are immutable in Python. Once created, the characters cannot be changed. Any operation that appears to modify a string actually creates a new one.
questions.detailed:
• s = "hello" — you cannot do s[0] = "H". This raises TypeError: str object does not support item assignment.
  • Operations like .upper(), .replace(), slicing — all return NEW strings.
  • The original string is untouched.
Key Point

Because strings are immutable, they are hashable and can be used as dictionary keys or set elements.

Common Trap

String concatenation in a loop (s += x) is O(n²) because each += creates a new string. Use "".join(list) instead for O(n) performance.
followups: Why are strings immutable?
What is the performance difference between += and join()?
Can strings be used as dictionary keys?

questions.q: What are the different string formatting methods in Python?
questions.short: Python supports three main string formatting methods: %-formatting, str.format(), and f-strings (preferred from Python 3.6+).
questions.detailed:
• %-formatting (old style): "Hello %s" % name. Works but verbose and error-prone.
  • str.format(): "Hello {}".format(name) or "Hello {name}".format(name=name). More readable.
  • f-strings (Python 3.6+): f"Hello {name}". Fastest, most readable, supports expressions.
  • Template strings: from string import Template; Template("Hello $name").substitute(name=name). Useful for user-provided format strings.
Key Point

f-strings are the fastest and most readable option. They evaluate expressions inline: f"Result: {2 + 2}" gives "Result: 4".

Pro Tip

f-strings support format specs: f"{value:.2f}" for 2 decimal places, f"{n:05d}" for zero-padding, f"{s!r}" for repr.
followups: What are f-strings?
What is the difference between format() and f-strings?
When would you use Template strings?

questions.q: What are f-strings and why are they preferred?
questions.short: f-strings (formatted string literals) embed expressions directly inside strings using curly braces. They are the fastest and most readable formatting method in Python 3.6+.
questions.detailed:
• Prefix the string with f or F: f"Hello {name}".
  • Any valid Python expression can go inside the braces: f"{2 + 2}", f"{obj.method()}".
  • Format specs work: f"{pi:.2f}" for 2 decimal places.
  • !r for repr, !s for str, !a for ascii representation.
  • Python 3.12 added f"{value=}" to print both the expression and its value for debugging.
Key Point

f-strings are faster than .format() and %-formatting because they are evaluated at compile time, not runtime.

Common Trap

f-strings cannot be used as docstrings and cannot span multiple logical lines unless the outer quotes span multiple lines. Also, backslashes inside {} are not allowed in Python < 3.12.
followups: What is the fastest string formatting method?
What is f-string debugging with =?
Can you use backslashes inside f-string expressions?

questions.q: Explain slicing in Python with examples.
questions.short: Slicing extracts a portion of a sequence using the syntax [start:stop:step]. It works on strings, lists, and tuples.
questions.detailed:
• s[start:stop]: Characters from index start up to (not including) stop.
  • s[start:]: From start to end.
  • s[:stop]: From beginning to stop.
  • s[::step]: Every step-th character.
  • s[::-1]: Reverse the string.
Negative indices count from the end: s[-1] is the last character, s[-3:] is the last 3 characters.
Key Point

Slicing never raises IndexError. If the indices are out of range, Python clamps them to valid values. s[0:100] on a 5-character string just returns the whole string.

Common Trap

The stop index is exclusive, not inclusive. s[1:4] gives characters at indices 1, 2, 3 — not 4.
followups: How do you reverse a string in Python?
What is the difference between s[1] and s[1:2]?
Does slicing work on lists?

questions.q: Differentiate between split() and partition() for strings.
questions.short: split() splits on a delimiter and returns a list. partition() splits on the first occurrence and always returns a 3-tuple: (before, delimiter, after).
questions.detailed:
• split(sep, maxsplit): Splits on sep and returns a list. If sep is not found, returns a list with the original string.
  • "a,b,c".split(",") gives ["a", "b", "c"].
  • partition(sep): Always returns exactly 3 parts: (before, sep, after). If sep is not found, returns (original, "", "").
  • "a,b,c".partition(",") gives ("a", ",", "b,c").
Key Point

Use partition() when you want to split on the FIRST occurrence only and always need exactly 3 parts. Use split() when you want all parts as a list.

Common Trap

split() without arguments splits on any whitespace and removes empty strings. split(" ") splits on a single space and keeps empty strings.
followups: What is rpartition()?
What is rsplit()?
What does split() return if the delimiter is not found?

questions.q: Differentiate between split() and join().
questions.short: split() converts a string to a list by breaking on a delimiter. join() is the reverse — it combines a list of strings into one string using a separator.
questions.detailed:
• split(sep): String method. "a,b,c".split(",") gives ["a","b","c"].
  • sep.join(iterable): String method called on the separator. ",".join(["a","b","c"]) gives "a,b,c".
They are inverses: "".join(s.split()) removes all whitespace from a string.
Key Point

join() is called on the SEPARATOR string, not on the list. This confuses many beginners. You write sep.join(lst), not lst.join(sep).

Common Trap

All elements passed to join() must be strings. If any element is an int or float, Python raises TypeError: sequence item 0: expected str instance, int found. Use ",".join(str(x) for x in lst).
followups: What is the time complexity of join() vs string concatenation in a loop?
Can join() work with non-string iterables?
What does ''.join(lst) do?

questions.q: Explain strip(), lstrip(), and rstrip() methods.
questions.short: strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace (or specified characters). lstrip() removes from the left only. rstrip() removes from the right only.
questions.detailed:
• " hello ".strip() gives "hello".
  • " hello ".lstrip() gives "hello ".
  • " hello ".rstrip() gives " hello".
  • You can pass characters to strip: "xxhelloxx".strip("x") gives "hello".
  • The argument is a SET of characters to remove, not a substring.
Key Point

When you pass a string argument to strip(), it removes any combination of those characters from both ends, not the exact string as a sequence.

Common Trap

"hello!?".strip("!?") removes both ! and ? from both ends. But "hello?!".strip("!?") also works because it removes any of those characters in any order, not the exact sequence "!?".
followups: What is the difference between strip() and replace()?
How do you remove a specific prefix or suffix in Python 3.9+?
Does strip() modify the original string?

questions.q: Differentiate between find() and index() methods.
questions.short: find() returns the index of the first occurrence, or -1 if not found. index() does the same but raises ValueError if not found.
questions.detailed:
• s.find(sub): Returns the lowest index of sub in s. Returns -1 if not found.
  • s.index(sub): Returns the lowest index of sub in s. Raises ValueError if not found.
  • Both accept optional start and end parameters: s.find(sub, start, end).
Key Point

Use find() when not finding the substring is an expected scenario. Use index() when absence is an error condition.

Common Trap

A common mistake is checking if s.index(sub) — this will crash if sub is not in s. Always use find() for safe checks, or wrap index() in a try-except.
followups: What is rfind() and rindex()?
How do you check if a substring exists without finding its index?
What is the in operator for strings?

questions.q: What does the replace() method do?
questions.short: replace(old, new, count) returns a new string with all (or count) occurrences of old replaced with new. The original string is unchanged.
questions.detailed:
• "hello".replace("l", "r") gives "herro".
  • "aaa".replace("a", "b", 2) gives "bba" — only first 2 occurrences.
  • If old is not found, the original string is returned unchanged.
  • Returns a new string — strings are immutable.
Key Point

To remove characters, replace with an empty string: "hello world".replace(" ", "") gives "helloworld".

Common Trap

If you need to replace multiple different substrings, calling replace() multiple times is fine for small strings. For large strings with many replacements, consider using re.sub() with a compiled pattern.
followups: How do you remove all spaces from a string?
What is the difference between replace() and re.sub()?
Does replace() modify the original string?

questions.q: Explain startswith() and endswith() methods.
questions.short: startswith(prefix) returns True if the string begins with prefix. endswith(suffix) returns True if it ends with suffix.
questions.detailed:
• "hello".startswith("he") gives True.
  • "hello".endswith("lo") gives True.
  • Both accept a tuple of strings: "hello".startswith(("he", "Hi")) — True if it starts with ANY of them.
  • Both accept optional start and end parameters to check within a slice.
Key Point

Passing a tuple to startswith() or endswith() is more efficient than chaining with or. It checks all options in one call.

Pro Tip

Python 3.9+ added str.removeprefix(prefix) and str.removesuffix(suffix) to cleanly remove a prefix/suffix if it exists, without affecting the string if it does not.
followups: Can you pass a tuple to startswith()?
What is removeprefix() in Python 3.9?
How is startswith() different from slicing s[:2] == 'he'?

questions.q: What are the different ways to concatenate strings in Python?
questions.short: Strings can be concatenated with +, repeated with *, joined with join(), or formatted with f-strings. Use join() for multiple strings in a loop.
questions.detailed:
• +: "hello" + " " + "world". Simple but inefficient in loops.
  • *: "ab" * 3 gives "ababab".
  • join(): " ".join(["hello", "world"]). Most efficient for multiple strings.
  • f-strings: f"{s1} {s2}". Best for readable formatting.
  • +=: In loops, creates a new string each time — O(n²) total.
Key Point

For concatenating many strings, always use "".join(list_of_strings). It is O(n) vs O(n²) for repeated +.

Common Trap

Using += in a loop to build a string is a classic performance mistake. Each += copies the entire string so far. With 1000 items, you copy ~500,000 characters total.
followups: Why is join() faster than + for string concatenation?
What is the time complexity of string concatenation?
How do you concatenate strings in Python 3?

questions.q: What is string interning in Python?
questions.short: String interning is an optimization where Python stores only one copy of identical string literals and reuses them. Interned strings share the same id().
questions.detailed:
• CPython automatically interns short strings that look like identifiers (letters, digits, underscores).
  • a = "hello"; b = "hello" — a is b is likely True because "hello" is interned.
  • a = "hello world"; b = "hello world" — a is b may be False (space prevents automatic interning).
  • You can force interning with sys.intern(s).
Key Point

String interning is an internal CPython optimisation. Do not rely on it in your code. Always use == to compare string values, not is.

Common Trap

Two strings with the same value may or may not be the same object depending on how they were created. Never use is for string comparison in production code.
followups: What is sys.intern()?
Why does is sometimes work for string comparison?
What is the difference between is and == for strings?

questions.q: What are the key characteristics of a list in Python?
questions.short: Lists are ordered, mutable, indexed collections that allow duplicate elements and can hold objects of any type.
questions.detailed:
• Ordered: Elements maintain insertion order.
  • Mutable: Elements can be added, removed, or changed after creation.
  • Indexed: Access by integer index (0-based). Supports negative indexing.
  • Heterogeneous: Can contain mixed types: [1, "hello", 3.14, True].
  • Allows duplicates: The same value can appear multiple times.
  • Dynamic size: Grows and shrinks as needed.
Key Point

Lists are implemented as dynamic arrays in CPython. Appending is O(1) amortized, but inserting or deleting at the front is O(n).

Pro Tip

Use a deque from collections if you need O(1) append and pop from both ends.
followups: What is the time complexity of list operations?
What is the difference between a list and an array?
When would you use a tuple instead of a list?

questions.q: Differentiate between append(), extend(), and insert() for lists.
questions.short: append() adds a single element to the end. extend() adds all elements from an iterable. insert(i, x) inserts x before index i.
questions.detailed:
• lst.append(x): Adds x as a single element to the end. O(1). [1,2].append([3,4]) gives [1,2,[3,4]].
  • lst.extend(iterable): Unpacks and adds each element. O(k). [1,2].extend([3,4]) gives [1,2,3,4].
  • lst.insert(i, x): Inserts x before index i. O(n) — shifts all subsequent elements.
Key Point

append() adds the object itself. extend() iterates and adds each item. This matters for nested lists: lst.append([3,4]) vs lst.extend([3,4]) produce different results.

Common Trap

[1,2] + [3,4] creates a NEW list. lst.extend([3,4]) modifies in-place. Do not confuse them when multiple variables reference the same list.
followups: What is the difference between append() and extend()?
What is the time complexity of insert()?
How do you add a list as a nested element?

questions.q: Compare remove(), pop(), and del for removing list elements.
questions.short: remove(x) removes the first occurrence of value x. pop(i) removes and returns the element at index i (default last). del removes by index or slice.
questions.detailed:
• lst.remove(x): Removes first occurrence of x. Raises ValueError if x not in list. O(n).
  • lst.pop(i): Removes and returns element at index i. Default is last element. O(n) for arbitrary index, O(1) for last.
  • del lst[i]: Removes element at index i. del lst[1:3] removes a slice. Does not return the value.
Key Point

pop() is the only one that returns the removed value. Use it when you need to process the removed element.

Common Trap

remove() removes by VALUE, not by index. If the list has duplicates, it only removes the first match. To remove all occurrences, use a list comprehension: lst = [x for x in lst if x != val].
followups: What is the difference between remove() and pop()?
How do you remove all occurrences of a value from a list?
What does del do?

questions.q: What is a list comprehension and how does it work?
questions.short: A list comprehension is a concise way to create a list from an iterable, optionally filtering with a condition: [expression for item in iterable if condition].
questions.detailed:
• Basic: [x**2 for x in range(10)] — squares of 0 to 9.
  • With condition: [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0] — even numbers.
  • Nested: [x for row in matrix for x in row] — flatten a 2D list.
  • With function: [len(w) for w in words] — word lengths.
Key Point

List comprehensions are faster than equivalent for loops because they are optimised in CPython. They also create the list in a single expression, making code more readable.

Common Trap

List comprehensions always return a list and build the entire list in memory. If you only need to iterate once, use a generator expression (x for x in ...) to save memory.
followups: What is a generator expression?
How does a list comprehension compare to a for loop in performance?
Can you have nested list comprehensions?

questions.q: How do you copy a list in Python? Explain shallow vs deep copy.
questions.short: Shallow copy creates a new list but nested objects are still shared. Deep copy creates a fully independent copy of all nested objects.
questions.detailed:
• Assignment (b = a): Not a copy. Both names reference the same list.
  • Shallow copy (b = a.copy() or b = a[:]): New outer list, but nested objects are shared references.
  • Deep copy (import copy; b = copy.deepcopy(a)): Fully independent — all nested objects are also copied.
Example: if a = [[1,2],[3,4]], a shallow copy b will share the inner lists. Modifying b[0].append(5) also changes a[0].
Key Point

For flat lists (no nested mutable objects), shallow copy is sufficient. Use deepcopy() only when you have nested lists, dicts, or other mutable objects.

Common Trap

People often forget that list() and a[:] are both shallow copies. Neither creates a fully independent nested structure.
followups: What is the difference between shallow and deep copy?
When would you use deepcopy()?
What is copy.copy() vs copy.deepcopy()?

questions.q: Differentiate between sort() and sorted().
questions.short: sort() modifies the list in-place and returns None. sorted() returns a new sorted list and works on any iterable.
questions.detailed:
• lst.sort(key=None, reverse=False): Sorts in-place. Returns None. Only works on lists.
  • sorted(iterable, key=None, reverse=False): Returns a new sorted list. Works on any iterable (strings, tuples, generators).
  • Both accept a key function: sorted(words, key=len) sorts by length.
  • Both accept reverse=True for descending order.
Key Point

Python's sort uses Timsort — O(n log n) worst case, O(n) best case (already sorted). It is a stable sort: equal elements maintain their original order.

Common Trap

A very common bug: result = lst.sort() gives you None, not the sorted list. sort() modifies in-place and returns None. Use sorted(lst) if you need a new list.
followups: What is Timsort?
What does stable sort mean?
How do you sort a list of dictionaries by a key?

questions.q: Differentiate between reverse() and reversed().
questions.short: reverse() modifies the list in-place and returns None. reversed() returns an iterator that yields elements in reverse order.
questions.detailed:
• lst.reverse(): Modifies the list in-place. Returns None. O(n).
  • reversed(lst): Returns a reverse iterator. Does not modify original. Works on sequences.
  • lst[::-1]: Returns a new reversed list via slicing. Does not modify original.
Key Point

Use lst[::-1] when you want a new reversed list. Use lst.reverse() when you want to reverse in-place. Use reversed() when you just need to iterate in reverse without creating a new list.

Common Trap

result = lst.reverse() stores None, not the reversed list. Same trap as sort().
followups: What is the difference between reverse() and [::-1]?
What does reversed() return?
How do you reverse a string in Python?

questions.q: What are nested lists and how do you access their elements?
questions.short: Nested lists are lists that contain other lists as elements. Access elements with multiple indices: matrix[row][col].
questions.detailed:
• Create: matrix = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]].
  • Access: matrix[0][1] gives 2 (row 0, col 1).
  • Iterate: for row in matrix: for x in row: ....
  • Flatten: [x for row in matrix for x in row].
Key Point

When creating a 2D list, avoid [[0]*3]*3. This creates 3 references to the SAME inner list. Modifying one row modifies all. Use: [[0]*3 for _ in range(3)] instead.

Common Trap

matrix = [[0]*3]*3; matrix[0][0] = 1 modifies all rows, not just the first. This is because *3 copies the reference, not the list itself.
followups: How do you create a 2D list in Python?
What is the difference between [[0]*3]*3 and [[0]*3 for _ in range(3)]?
How do you flatten a nested list?

questions.q: What is the time complexity of common list operations?
questions.short: List operation complexities: append O(1), pop from end O(1), insert/delete at index O(n), index access O(1), membership (in) O(n).
questions.detailed:
• lst[i]: O(1) — direct index access.
  • lst.append(x): O(1) amortized — occasional resize is O(n) but rare.
  • lst.pop(): O(1) — remove from end.
  • lst.pop(0) or lst.insert(0, x): O(n) — shifts all elements.
  • x in lst: O(n) — linear scan.
  • lst.sort(): O(n log n).
  • len(lst): O(1) — stored separately.
Key Point

If you need O(1) insert/delete at both ends, use collections.deque. If you need O(1) membership check, use a set.

Pro Tip

In interviews, always mention the time complexity of your list operations. Knowing that list.pop(0) is O(n) while deque.popleft() is O(1) shows depth.
followups: What data structure gives O(1) insert and delete from both ends?
What is the time complexity of in for a set?
What is amortized O(1)?

questions.q: What is list unpacking in Python?
questions.short: List unpacking assigns list elements to individual variables in a single statement. The extended unpacking syntax (*) captures remaining elements.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: a, b, c = [1, 2, 3] — assigns 1 to a, 2 to b, 3 to c.
  • Extended: first, *rest = [1, 2, 3, 4] — first=1, rest=[2,3,4].
  • Swap: a, b = b, a — elegant Python swap without a temp variable.
  • In for loops: for x, y in [(1,2),(3,4)]: ... — unpack each tuple.
Key Point

The starred expression *rest captures any number of elements. It can appear in the middle: first, *middle, last = lst.

Common Trap

The number of variables must match the number of elements unless you use * unpacking. a, b = [1, 2, 3] raises ValueError: too many values to unpack.
followups: What is extended unpacking?
How do you swap two variables in Python?
Can you unpack a dictionary?

questions.q: Differentiate between tuples and lists. When would you use a tuple over a list?
questions.short: Lists are mutable, ordered, and use []. Tuples are immutable, ordered, and use (). Use tuples for fixed data, function return values, and dictionary keys.
questions.detailed:
• List: mutable — supports append, remove, sort. Slightly more memory due to over-allocation.
  • Tuple: immutable — no add/remove/change. Faster iteration and smaller memory footprint.
Use tuples when:
  • Data should not change (coordinates, RGB values, DB records).
  • Using as dictionary keys or set elements (tuples are hashable).
  • Returning multiple values from a function.
  • Performance matters in tight loops.
Key Point

Tuples are slightly faster than lists for iteration and creation. CPython can optimise immutable structures more aggressively.

Common Trap

A single-element tuple needs a trailing comma: (42,). Without it, (42) is just 42 in parentheses, not a tuple.
followups: Are tuples hashable?
Can you use a tuple as a dictionary key?
What methods do tuples have?

questions.q: What is tuple unpacking and how is it used?
questions.short: Tuple unpacking assigns tuple elements to individual variables. Python uses it extensively for multiple assignment, swapping, and loop unpacking.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: x, y = (3, 4) — x=3, y=4.
  • Swap: a, b = b, a — Python creates a tuple on the right before assigning.
  • Function return: def minmax(lst): return min(lst), max(lst); lo, hi = minmax([3,1,4]).
  • Loop: for k, v in d.items(): — unpack each key-value pair.
  • Extended: first, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4).
Key Point

When a function returns multiple values, Python actually returns a tuple. The unpacking makes it look like multiple returns.

Pro Tip

You can use _ as a throwaway variable: _, y = (1, 2) — you only care about y.
followups: How does Python implement multiple return values?
What is the _ variable in unpacking?
What is extended unpacking?

questions.q: Why are tuples immutable? What is the benefit?
questions.short: Tuples are immutable because they represent fixed collections of data. Immutability makes them hashable, faster, and safer to use as dictionary keys or function arguments.
questions.detailed:
• Once created, tuple elements cannot be added, removed, or changed.
  • Immutability guarantees data integrity — useful for configuration values, constants, records.
  • Tuples are hashable (if their elements are hashable), enabling use as dict keys and set elements.
  • CPython can optimise tuple storage since the size is fixed at creation.
Key Point

Immutability does not mean the variables holding the tuple cannot be reassigned. t = (1,2); t = (3,4) is valid — you are rebinding t, not modifying the tuple.

Common Trap

A tuple CAN contain mutable objects (like lists). If it does, the tuple itself is still "immutable" (you cannot reassign its elements), but the mutable objects inside can still be changed.
followups: Can a tuple contain mutable objects?
Why are tuples hashable but lists are not?
What is the difference between immutability and constancy?

questions.q: How do you create a single element tuple in Python?
questions.short: A single-element tuple requires a trailing comma: (42,). Without the comma, (42) is just the integer 42 in parentheses.
questions.detailed:
• (42,): A tuple containing 42. type((42,)) is tuple.
  • (42): Just the integer 42. type((42)) is int.
  • The comma, not the parentheses, makes a tuple.
  • Empty tuple: () — parentheses without comma is valid here.
Key Point

Parentheses are not what makes a tuple — the comma is. You can even write a tuple without parentheses: t = 1, or t = 1, 2, 3.

Common Trap

This is a very common Python interview question. Many beginners assume (42) is a tuple because it has parentheses. Always add the trailing comma for a single-element tuple.
followups: How do you create an empty tuple?
What makes a tuple in Python — the parens or the comma?
Can you create a tuple without parentheses?

questions.q: What are named tuples and what advantage do they provide?
questions.short: Named tuples are tuples with named fields. They make code more readable while keeping tuple efficiency. Created with collections.namedtuple or typing.NamedTuple.
questions.detailed:
• from collections import namedtuple.
  • Point = namedtuple("Point", ["x", "y"]).
  • p = Point(3, 4); access as p.x or p[0].
  • Immutable like regular tuples. Supports all tuple operations.
  • typing.NamedTuple provides a class-based syntax with type hints.
Key Point

Named tuples are more self-documenting than regular tuples. p.x is clearer than p[0]. They also have a _asdict() method to convert to a dict.

Pro Tip

If you need mutability, use Python 3.7+ dataclasses instead of named tuples. Named tuples are great for lightweight, immutable data structures.
followups: What is the difference between namedtuple and dataclass?
How do you convert a namedtuple to a dict?
What methods do named tuples have?

questions.q: Can tuples contain mutable objects? What happens when they do?
questions.short: Yes, a tuple can contain mutable objects like lists. The tuple itself is immutable (you cannot reassign its elements), but the mutable objects inside can still be changed.
questions.detailed:
• t = ([1, 2], [3, 4]) — tuple of two lists.
  • t[0].append(5) is valid — you are mutating the list object, not reassigning the tuple element.
  • t[0] = [1, 2, 5] raises TypeError — you cannot reassign the tuple slot.
Key Point

A tuple containing a list is NOT hashable, even though plain tuples are. Hashability requires all elements to be hashable, and lists are not.

Common Trap

This is a classic interview question. "Can a tuple be changed?" — the answer is: the tuple itself cannot be changed, but mutable objects inside it can be mutated.
followups: Is a tuple with a list inside hashable?
What is the difference between mutation and reassignment?
When is a tuple not hashable?

questions.q: Why do tuples have only two methods (count and index)?
questions.short: Tuples only have count() and index() because they are immutable. There is no need for append, remove, sort, or other mutating methods.
questions.detailed:
• t.count(x): Returns how many times x appears in the tuple.
  • t.index(x): Returns the index of the first occurrence of x. Raises ValueError if not found.
  • All other list methods (append, extend, remove, pop, sort, reverse) would mutate the tuple, which is forbidden by design.
Key Point

Tuples deliberately have a minimal API. If you find yourself needing more operations, you may want a list instead.

Pro Tip

You can still use built-in functions on tuples: len(t), min(t), max(t), sum(t), sorted(t) (returns a new list).
followups: What methods do tuples have?
Can you sort a tuple?
How do you convert a tuple to a list?

questions.q: What is a set in Python? What are its properties?
questions.short: A set is an unordered, mutable collection of unique, hashable elements. It provides O(1) average membership testing using hashing.
questions.detailed:
• Unordered: No guaranteed order. Indexing is not supported.
  • Unique: Duplicate elements are automatically removed.
  • Mutable: Elements can be added or removed (but elements themselves must be immutable/hashable).
  • Fast: x in s is O(1) average, compared to O(n) for lists.
Key Point

Sets are ideal when you need to eliminate duplicates from a collection or check membership frequently.

Common Trap

You cannot create a set with s = {}. That creates an empty dict. Use s = set() for an empty set.
followups: How do you create an empty set?
What is the time complexity of set operations?
What is a frozenset?

questions.q: Explain set operations: union, intersection, difference, and symmetric difference.
questions.short: Set operations: union (|) combines elements, intersection (&) finds common elements, difference (-) removes common elements, symmetric difference (^) keeps elements in one but not both.
questions.detailed:
• Union (s1 | s2 or s1.union(s2)): All elements from both sets.
  • Intersection (s1 & s2 or s1.intersection(s2)): Only elements present in both.
  • Difference (s1 - s2 or s1.difference(s2)): Elements in s1 not in s2.
  • Symmetric difference (s1 ^ s2 or s1.symmetric_difference(s2)): Elements in either set but not both.
  • Subset/Superset: s1.issubset(s2), s1.issuperset(s2), s1.isdisjoint(s2).
Key Point

Set operations return new sets. The original sets are unchanged. Use the update versions (|=, &=, etc.) to modify in-place.

Pro Tip

A common interview use case: find elements in one list but not another — set(list1) - set(list2).
followups: What is the difference between union() and update()?
How do you find common elements between two lists efficiently?
What is a disjoint set?

questions.q: Differentiate between a set and a list.
questions.short: Sets are unordered and contain only unique elements. Lists are ordered and allow duplicates. Sets have O(1) membership check; lists have O(n).
questions.detailed:
• Ordering: Lists are ordered; sets are not.
  • Duplicates: Lists allow duplicates; sets automatically remove them.
  • Indexing: Lists support lst[0]; sets do not.
  • Membership: x in set is O(1); x in list is O(n).
  • Mutability: Both are mutable, but set elements must be hashable.
Key Point

Use sets when you need uniqueness or fast membership testing. Use lists when order matters or you need duplicates.

Pro Tip

Convert a list to a set to remove duplicates: list(set(lst)). Note that this loses the original order. Use dict.fromkeys(lst) to deduplicate while preserving order (Python 3.7+).
followups: How do you deduplicate a list while preserving order?
What is the time complexity of x in list vs x in set?
When would you choose a list over a set?

questions.q: What is a frozenset and when would you use it?
questions.short: A frozenset is an immutable version of a set. Once created, elements cannot be added or removed. Frozensets are hashable and can be used as dict keys or set elements.
questions.detailed:
• Create: fs = frozenset([1, 2, 3]).
  • Supports all read-only set operations: union, intersection, membership testing.
  • Does NOT support: add(), discard(), remove(), update().
  • Hashable: hash(frozenset([1,2,3])) works.
  • Can be used as a dict key: d = {frozenset([1,2]): "value"}.
Key Point

Use frozenset when you need set semantics (unique elements, fast lookup) but the collection must be immutable — for example, as a dictionary key representing a group of items.

Common Trap

Regular sets are not hashable and cannot be used as dict keys. If you need a set as a key, convert it to a frozenset first.
followups: When would you use a frozenset?
Is frozenset faster than set?
Can a frozenset contain mutable objects?

questions.q: Differentiate between discard() and remove() in sets.
questions.short: discard(x) removes x if present, does nothing if absent. remove(x) removes x but raises KeyError if x is not in the set.
questions.detailed:
• s.remove(x): Removes x. Raises KeyError if x is not in the set.
  • s.discard(x): Removes x if present. Does nothing if x is absent. Never raises an error.
Key Point

Prefer discard() when you are not sure if the element exists and do not want to handle exceptions. Use remove() when absence should be treated as an error.

Common Trap

This is the same pattern as dict.pop(key) (raises KeyError) vs dict.pop(key, default) (safe). Know both patterns.
followups: What is the set equivalent of list's remove()?
How do you safely remove an element from a set?
What does pop() do on a set?

questions.q: What is a set comprehension?
questions.short: A set comprehension creates a set using the syntax {expression for item in iterable if condition}, automatically deduplicating the results.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: {x**2 for x in range(10)} — set of squares (no duplicates).
  • With condition: {x for x in lst if x > 0} — positive elements as a set.
  • Deduplication: {len(w) for w in words} — unique word lengths.
Key Point

Set comprehensions are perfect when you want the unique results of some operation without writing an explicit loop and calling add() each time.

Pro Tip

The syntax is similar to list comprehension but uses curly braces {} instead of brackets []. Do not confuse with dict comprehension which has key: value syntax.
followups: How is a set comprehension different from a list comprehension?
What is a dict comprehension?
Can you have conditions in a set comprehension?

questions.q: What is a dictionary in Python? What are its key characteristics?
questions.short: A dictionary is an ordered (Python 3.7+), mutable collection of key-value pairs. Keys must be unique and hashable. Values can be any type.
questions.detailed:
• Key-value pairs: Each entry maps a unique key to a value.
  • Ordered: Maintains insertion order since Python 3.7.
  • Mutable: Keys and values can be added, changed, or removed.
  • Keys must be hashable: strings, ints, tuples are valid keys. Lists and dicts are not.
  • O(1) average lookup: Accessing a value by key is very fast.
Key Point

Dictionaries are implemented as hash tables internally. This gives O(1) average time for get, set, and delete operations.

Common Trap

Using {} creates an empty dict, not an empty set. An empty set must be created with set().
followups: What is a hash table?
What types can be used as dictionary keys?
What is the time complexity of dictionary operations?

questions.q: Why use dict.get() instead of direct key access with []?
questions.short: dict[key] raises KeyError if the key is missing. dict.get(key, default) returns None (or a default) instead of raising an error.
questions.detailed:
• d["key"]: Direct access. Raises KeyError if key does not exist.
  • d.get("key"): Returns None if key is absent.
  • d.get("key", "default"): Returns "default" if key is absent.
  • Use get() whenever absence is a normal expected scenario.
Key Point

The get() method is the safest way to access dictionary values when you are not sure if the key exists.

Common Trap

A common mistake is: count = d["word"] + 1 — this crashes if "word" is not yet in the dict. The correct pattern is: count = d.get("word", 0) + 1.
followups: What is setdefault()?
What is defaultdict?
How do you count word frequencies using a dict?

questions.q: How do you merge two dictionaries in Python?
questions.short: Two dicts can be merged using the | operator (Python 3.9+), the ** unpacking operator, or dict.update(). The last writer wins on duplicate keys.
questions.detailed:
• Python 3.9+ merge operator: d1 | d2 returns a new merged dict.
  • Unpacking: {**d1, **d2} — works in Python 3.5+. d2 overwrites d1 on conflicts.
  • update(): d1.update(d2) — modifies d1 in-place. d2 values overwrite d1.
  • For loop: Iterate and manually assign — verbose but gives full control.
Key Point

In all merge methods, if both dicts have the same key, the second dict's value wins. The order of operands matters.

Common Trap

dict.update() modifies the original dict in-place and returns None. result = d1.update(d2) gives you None, not the merged dict.
followups: What is the | operator for dicts?
What happens when both dicts have the same key?
What does update() return?

questions.q: What is a dictionary comprehension?
questions.short: A dictionary comprehension creates a dict from an iterable using {key: value for item in iterable if condition}.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: {k: v for k, v in pairs}.
  • From keys: {word: len(word) for word in words}.
  • Invert a dict: {v: k for k, v in d.items()}.
  • Filter: {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > 0}.
  • Transform: {k: v*2 for k, v in d.items()}.
Key Point

Dict comprehensions are concise and fast. They are especially useful for transforming, filtering, or inverting dictionaries in one expression.

Common Trap

When inverting a dict with {v: k for k, v in d.items()}, if multiple keys have the same value, only the last one survives. The original must have unique values for a clean inversion.
followups: How do you invert a dictionary?
What is the difference between dict comprehension and dict()?
Can you have conditions in a dict comprehension?

questions.q: Explain keys(), values(), and items() methods.
questions.short: keys() returns all keys, values() returns all values, items() returns all key-value pairs as (key, value) tuples. All return view objects, not lists.
questions.detailed:
• d.keys(): Returns a view of all keys. dict_keys(["a", "b"]).
  • d.values(): Returns a view of all values. dict_values([1, 2]).
  • d.items(): Returns a view of (key, value) pairs. Used frequently in for loops.
View objects are live: They reflect changes to the dict in real time. If you add a key to the dict, the view updates automatically.
Key Point

Iterate over a dict's items with: for k, v in d.items():. This is the most Pythonic way to loop over key-value pairs.

Common Trap

Do not modify a dict while iterating over it — this raises RuntimeError: dictionary changed size during iteration. Iterate over a copy: for k in list(d.keys()):.
followups: What is a dict view object?
What happens if you modify a dict while iterating?
How do you get all keys as a list?

questions.q: Differentiate between pop() and popitem() in dictionaries.
questions.short: pop(key) removes and returns the value for a specific key. popitem() removes and returns the last inserted (key, value) pair.
questions.detailed:
• d.pop("key"): Removes and returns value for "key". Raises KeyError if absent.
  • d.pop("key", default): Returns default if key not found — no error.
  • d.popitem(): Removes and returns the last inserted (key, value) pair as a tuple. Raises KeyError if dict is empty.
  • Useful for iterating and consuming a dict: while d: k, v = d.popitem().
Key Point

popitem() is guaranteed to return the last inserted pair in Python 3.7+ (LIFO order). In older Python versions, the order was arbitrary.

Common Trap

d.pop("key") without a default raises KeyError. Always provide a default when key absence is expected: d.pop("key", None).
followups: What is the difference between pop() and popitem()?
How do you safely remove a key from a dict?
What order does popitem() use?

questions.q: What does the update() method do in a dictionary?
