questions.short: update() merges another dict (or iterable of key-value pairs) into the current dict in-place. Existing keys are overwritten by new values.
questions.detailed:
• d.update({"b": 2, "c": 3}): Adds/overwrites keys from the argument.
  • d.update(b=2, c=3): Keyword argument form.
  • d.update([("b", 2), ("c", 3)]): Iterable of pairs.
  • Returns None — modifies in-place.
Key Point

update() is equivalent to d |= other in Python 3.9+. Both modify d in-place.

Common Trap

result = d.update(other) stores None. update() is in-place and returns None. Use d | other if you want a new merged dict.
followups: What is the difference between update() and |?
Does update() return the new dict?
What happens with duplicate keys in update()?

questions.q: What is defaultdict and how is it different from a regular dict?
questions.short: defaultdict is a dict subclass from the collections module that automatically creates a default value for missing keys instead of raising KeyError.
questions.detailed:
• Import: from collections import defaultdict.
  • d = defaultdict(int): Missing keys return 0.
  • d = defaultdict(list): Missing keys return [].
  • d = defaultdict(str): Missing keys return "".
  • d["key"] += 1: Works without initialising — defaultdict auto-creates the key with value 0.
Key Point

defaultdict is perfect for frequency counting, grouping, and building nested data structures without checking if keys exist first.

Pro Tip

The argument to defaultdict is a callable (factory function). You can use a lambda for custom defaults: defaultdict(lambda: "unknown").
followups: How is defaultdict different from dict.get()?
What is Counter in collections?
Can you use a lambda as the default factory?

questions.q: What is OrderedDict and when would you use it?
questions.short: OrderedDict is a dict subclass that remembered insertion order. In Python 3.7+, regular dicts also maintain insertion order, making OrderedDict less necessary.
questions.detailed:
• Import: from collections import OrderedDict.
  • In Python 2 and early Python 3, regular dicts did not guarantee order. OrderedDict was essential.
  • Since Python 3.7, regular dicts guarantee insertion order, so OrderedDict is mostly redundant.
  • OrderedDict still has two extra methods: move_to_end(key) and popitem(last=True/False).
  • OrderedDict considers order when comparing equality: two OrderedDicts with same items in different order are NOT equal.
Key Point

In modern Python (3.7+), prefer regular dicts unless you need OrderedDict-specific features like move_to_end() or order-sensitive equality.

Common Trap

Regular dict equality ignores order: {"a":1,"b":2} == {"b":2,"a":1} is True. But OrderedDict equality considers order — they would not be equal if order differs.
followups: When would you still use OrderedDict in Python 3.7+?
What is move_to_end()?
How does dict equality work?

questions.q: What does setdefault() do in a dictionary?
questions.short: setdefault(key, default) returns the value for key if it exists. If it does not, it inserts key with the default value and returns that default.
questions.detailed:
• d.setdefault("key", []): If "key" exists, returns its value. If not, sets d["key"] = [] and returns [].
  • Common pattern for grouping: d.setdefault(group_key, []).append(item).
  • Unlike get(), setdefault() actually INSERTS the default into the dict.
Key Point

setdefault() is useful when you want to ensure a key exists in the dict with a default value, and also get the current value in one call.

Common Trap

d.get("key", []) does NOT insert the default. d.setdefault("key", []) DOES insert it. This is the key difference.
followups: What is the difference between get() and setdefault()?
When would you use setdefault() over defaultdict?
Does setdefault() overwrite existing values?

questions.q: How does iteration work over a dictionary?
questions.short: By default, iterating over a dict yields its keys. Use .values() for values or .items() for key-value pairs.
questions.detailed:
• for k in d:: Iterates over keys (same as for k in d.keys():).
  • for v in d.values():: Iterates over values.
  • for k, v in d.items():: Iterates over (key, value) tuples — most common pattern.
  • Order is guaranteed to be insertion order in Python 3.7+.
Key Point

Always use d.items() when you need both the key and value inside the loop. It is cleaner than accessing d[k] inside a key-only loop.

Common Trap

Do not modify a dict while iterating — this raises a RuntimeError. If you need to delete keys, collect them first: keys_to_delete = [k for k in d if condition]; for k in keys_to_delete: del d[k].
followups: How do you iterate over a dict in sorted key order?
What is the default iteration order of a dict?
How do you safely delete keys while iterating?

questions.q: How does positive and negative indexing work in Python?
questions.short: Positive indexing starts at 0 from the left. Negative indexing starts at -1 from the right, making it easy to access elements from the end.
questions.detailed:
• For a sequence s = "Python":
  • Positive: s[0]="P", s[1]="y", s[5]="n".
  • Negative: s[-1]="n", s[-2]="o", s[-6]="P".
  • Positive index i and negative index i-len(s) refer to the same element.
Key Point

Negative indexing is a Python feature, not universal. It is equivalent to s[len(s) + i] — so s[-1] is s[len(s)-1].

Common Trap

Accessing an index beyond the valid range raises IndexError. But slicing never raises IndexError — out-of-range slice indices are silently clamped.
followups: What is the last valid positive index for a string of length n?
How does slicing handle out-of-range indices?
What raises IndexError?

questions.q: Which data types support indexing and slicing?
questions.short: Indexing and slicing work on all sequence types: str, list, tuple, range, and bytes. Dicts and sets do not support indexing.
questions.detailed:
• str: "hello"[1] gives "e".
  • list: [1,2,3][0] gives 1.
  • tuple: (1,2,3)[-1] gives 3.
  • range: range(10)[3] gives 3.
  • bytes: b"hello"[0] gives 104 (integer).
  • dict and set: No integer indexing — unordered or key-based.
Key Point

All sequence types support the full slice syntax. The key requirement is that the type maintains a defined order with integer positions.

Common Trap

Indexing a dict returns the value for that key, not by position. d[0] means "get value for key 0", not "get first element".
followups: Can you index a set?
Can you index a dict?
What is the difference between a sequence and a mapping?

questions.q: How do you reverse a sequence using slicing?
questions.short: Use slice with step -1: s[::-1]. This works for strings, lists, and tuples and returns a new reversed sequence.
questions.detailed:
• s[::-1]: Reverses the sequence. Start and stop default to the full range; step -1 goes backward.
  • "Python"[::-1] gives "nohtyP".
  • [1,2,3,4][::-1] gives [4,3,2,1].
  • Does not modify the original — returns a new object.
Key Point

For lists, you can also use lst.reverse() (in-place) or reversed(lst) (returns an iterator). For strings, [::-1] is the only clean option.

Pro Tip

In coding interviews, reversing with [::-1] is concise. But if asked to implement reversal from scratch, use a two-pointer approach.
followups: What is the difference between s[::-1] and reversed(s)?
How do you reverse a list in-place?
What does s[::2] do?

questions.q: Explain the full slice syntax: start, stop, step.
questions.short: The full slice syntax is s[start:stop:step]. start is inclusive, stop is exclusive, step is the increment. All three are optional.
questions.detailed:
• s[start:stop]: From start up to (not including) stop.
  • s[::step]: Every step-th element from the whole sequence.
  • s[1:8:2]: Elements at indices 1, 3, 5, 7.
  • s[::-1]: Reverse (step -1, defaults start/stop to ends).
  • s[::2]: Every second element.
  • Defaults: start=0 (or end for negative step), stop=len(s) (or beginning for negative step), step=1.
Key Point

When using a negative step, the defaults for start and stop flip: start defaults to the last element and stop defaults to before the first.

Common Trap

s[0:5:-1] gives an empty result because with step -1, the start (0) is to the left of stop (5), and we are going right-to-left. To reverse a slice, use s[5:0:-1] or s[5::-1].
followups: What does s[1:-1] do?
How does slicing handle negative step?
What is the difference between s[2] and s[2:3]?

questions.q: What types of conditional statements exist in Python?
questions.short: Python has if, if-else, if-elif-else, and the ternary (conditional) expression. There is no switch statement (Python 3.10+ has match-case).
questions.detailed:
• if condition:: Executes if condition is truthy.
  • if ... else:: Two-way branch.
  • if ... elif ... else:: Multi-way branch. Can have multiple elif blocks.
  • Ternary: x if condition else y: Inline conditional expression.
  • match-case (Python 3.10+): Structural pattern matching, similar to switch.
Key Point

Python evaluates conditions using truthiness, not strict boolean comparison. Any non-zero, non-empty, non-None value is truthy.

Pro Tip

Python does not have a switch statement before 3.10. The Pythonic alternative is a dict mapping: actions = {"a": fn_a, "b": fn_b}; actions.get(key, default_fn)().
followups: What is the ternary operator in Python?
What is match-case in Python 3.10?
How do you simulate a switch statement in Python?

questions.q: Explain truthy and falsy values in Python with examples.
questions.short: Truthy values evaluate to True in a boolean context. Falsy values evaluate to False. Python has clear rules about which values are falsy.
questions.detailed:
Falsy values in Python:
  • None
  • False
  • 0, 0.0, 0j (numeric zero of any type)
  • "" (empty string)
  • [] (empty list), () (empty tuple), {} (empty dict/set)
  • set(), range(0)
  • Objects whose __bool__() returns False or __len__() returns 0.
Everything else is truthy.
Key Point

Use truthiness for clean conditional checks: if lst: instead of if len(lst) > 0:. Both work, but the first is more Pythonic.

Common Trap

if x: and if x is not None: are NOT the same. 0, "", and [] are all falsy but not None. Use is not None when you specifically want to check for None.
followups: What is the difference between if x: and if x is not None:?
What is bool()?
How do you make a custom class falsy?

questions.q: What is the ternary (conditional) operator in Python?
questions.short: The ternary operator evaluates to one of two values based on a condition: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false.
questions.detailed:
• Syntax: result = x if condition else y.
  • Example: label = "even" if n % 2 == 0 else "odd".
  • Can be nested (but avoid for readability): "positive" if n > 0 else "negative" if n < 0 else "zero".
  • Works in assignments, function arguments, list comprehensions.
Key Point

The ternary operator is a single expression, not a statement. This means it can be used inside other expressions like list comprehensions or function arguments.

Common Trap

Deeply nested ternary expressions are hard to read. If you find yourself nesting more than two levels, use a regular if-elif-else block instead.
followups: Can you nest ternary operators?
What is the ternary operator called in Python?
Is the ternary operator a statement or an expression?

questions.q: When would you use nested if-else vs if-elif ladder?
questions.short: Use if-elif ladder for multiple mutually exclusive conditions based on the same variable. Use nested if-else when conditions are independent and one depends on another.
questions.detailed:
• if-elif ladder: When checking the same variable against multiple values. Clean and efficient — stops at first match.
  • Nested if-else: When a second condition only makes sense if the first is true. Adds another level of indentation.
  • Prefer the dict dispatch pattern for many branches on the same variable.
Key Point

An if-elif chain is evaluated top-to-bottom and stops at the first True condition. This makes it efficient for mutually exclusive cases.

Common Trap

Avoid deep nesting (more than 2-3 levels). Use early returns, guard clauses, or dict dispatch to flatten deeply nested conditions.
followups: What is a guard clause?
How do you simulate a switch with a dict?
What is the match-case statement?

questions.q: Is the else block mandatory in an if statement?
questions.short: No, the else block is optional in an if statement. It only runs when the if condition is False and no elif was True.
questions.detailed:
• An if block can stand alone without else.
  • The else block runs when ALL preceding conditions are False.
  • In an if-elif-else chain, only one block executes.
  • The else block on a for/while loop is different — it runs when the loop completes without hitting break.
Key Point

The else on a loop is a Python-specific feature that trips up developers from other languages. It is NOT "else if the loop did nothing" — it is "else if the loop was not broken out of".

Common Trap

The else on a try-except block is also different — it runs only when NO exception was raised. Python's else is context-dependent.
followups: What does else do on a for loop?
What does else do on a try-except block?
Can if have multiple else blocks?

questions.q: What is short-circuit evaluation in Python?
questions.short: Short-circuit evaluation means Python stops evaluating an and/or expression as soon as the result is determined, without evaluating the remaining operands.
questions.detailed:
• and: Stops and returns the first falsy value. If all are truthy, returns the last value.
  • or: Stops and returns the first truthy value. If all are falsy, returns the last value.
  • Example: x = None; x and x.method() — safe because x.method() is never called when x is None.
  • Example: name = user_input or "default" — common idiom for default values.
Key Point

Short-circuit evaluation is not just an optimisation — it enables safe conditional access patterns. lst and lst[0] safely gets the first element only if the list is non-empty.

Common Trap

False and expensive_function() will NOT call expensive_function(). True or expensive_function() will also NOT call it. Side effects inside short-circuited expressions will be skipped.
followups: What does and return when both operands are truthy?
What is the common or idiom for default values?
Does Python have lazy evaluation?

questions.q: Differentiate between for and while loops in Python.
questions.short: for loops iterate over a sequence or iterable a known number of times. while loops repeat as long as a condition is True — used when the number of iterations is unknown.
questions.detailed:
• for: Iterates over an iterable (list, range, string, dict, etc.). Cleaner for sequence traversal.
  • while: Repeats as long as condition is True. Use when iterations depend on runtime state.
  • Use for: iterating over collections, known number of iterations.
  • Use while: reading until a sentinel value, retrying until success, game loops.
Key Point

In Python, the for loop is preferred in most cases because it is safer (cannot accidentally loop forever) and more readable.

Common Trap

A while True: loop runs forever unless there is a break inside. Always ensure the loop has a clear exit condition.
followups: What is an infinite loop?
Can a for loop run forever?
What is the else clause on a loop?

questions.q: Differentiate between break, continue, and pass statements.
questions.short: break exits the loop immediately. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and goes to the next. pass is a no-op placeholder that does nothing.
questions.detailed:
• break: Exits the nearest enclosing loop entirely. The loop's else block (if any) is skipped.
  • continue: Skips the rest of the current loop body and starts the next iteration.
  • pass: Does nothing. Used as a placeholder for empty blocks (empty function, class, if body).
Key Point

break skips the loop's else clause. continue does not. This distinction matters if you use the loop-else pattern.

Common Trap

pass is not the same as continue. pass does nothing and the loop continues normally from the next statement. continue skips to the next iteration.
followups: What happens to the else clause when break is used?
When would you use pass?
Can you use break in a nested loop to break the outer loop?

questions.q: Explain the range() function and its use with for loops.
questions.short: range(stop) generates integers from 0 to stop-1. range(start, stop) or range(start, stop, step) give more control. It returns a lazy range object, not a list.
questions.detailed:
• range(5): 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
  • range(2, 8): 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7.
  • range(0, 10, 2): 0, 2, 4, 6, 8.
  • range(10, 0, -1): 10, 9, 8, ..., 1.
  • range() returns a lazy object — it does not generate all values in memory at once.
Key Point

range() is memory-efficient. range(1000000) uses a tiny fixed amount of memory regardless of size, because it generates values on demand.

Common Trap

range(5) is a range object, not a list. Use list(range(5)) to get a list. Also, the stop value is exclusive: range(1, 5) gives 1, 2, 3, 4 — not 5.
followups: Is range() lazy or eager?
How do you create a list from range?
How do you iterate in reverse with range?

questions.q: What are iterables and iterators in Python?
questions.short: An iterable is any object you can loop over. An iterator is an object that generates values one at a time using next(). All iterators are iterables, but not all iterables are iterators.
questions.detailed:
• Iterable: Has __iter__() method. Can be passed to for or iter(). Examples: list, str, tuple, dict, set, range.
  • Iterator: Has both __iter__() and __next__(). Generates values one at a time. Examples: file objects, generators, map(), filter().
  • Converting: it = iter(lst) creates an iterator from a list. next(it) gets the next value.
Key Point

When Python executes for x in obj:, it calls iter(obj) to get an iterator, then repeatedly calls next() until StopIteration is raised.

Common Trap

Iterators are exhausted after one pass. Once a generator or iterator is consumed, iterating over it again gives nothing. Lists can be iterated multiple times because they are iterables, not iterators.
followups: What is the iterator protocol?
What is the difference between __iter__ and __next__?
What is a generator?

questions.q: Explain enumerate() and zip() functions with examples.
questions.short: enumerate() yields (index, value) pairs. zip() pairs up elements from multiple iterables. Both return lazy iterators.
questions.detailed:
• enumerate(lst): Yields (0, lst[0]), (1, lst[1]), ... Use enumerate(lst, start=1) to start from 1.
  • zip(a, b): Pairs elements: (a[0],b[0]), (a[1],b[1]), ... Stops at the shortest iterable.
  • zip(*matrix): Transposes a 2D list.
  • zip_longest from itertools pads with a fill value instead of stopping at shortest.
Key Point

enumerate() is the Pythonic way to loop with an index. Never write for i in range(len(lst)): when for i, v in enumerate(lst): is available.

Common Trap

zip() stops at the shortest iterable silently. If your lists have different lengths, the extra elements are ignored. Use itertools.zip_longest() to keep all elements.
followups: What does zip() return?
How do you loop with an index in Python?
What is itertools.zip_longest()?

questions.q: How do nested loops work? Give an example.
questions.short: Nested loops are loops inside other loops. The inner loop runs fully for each iteration of the outer loop. Time complexity multiplies: O(n) outer * O(m) inner = O(n*m).
questions.detailed:
• Basic pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(m): ....
  • Used for: matrix traversal, generating pairs, comparing all elements.
  • Break in inner loop only exits the inner loop, not the outer.
  • To break out of nested loops, use a flag variable, a function with return, or (rarely) try-except.
Key Point

Each nesting level multiplies the time complexity. Three nested loops over n elements = O(n³). Always look for ways to reduce nesting.

Pro Tip

To break out of nested loops cleanly, wrap them in a function and use return instead of break. This is cleaner than using flag variables.
followups: How do you break out of a nested loop?
What is the time complexity of nested loops?
Can you use continue in a nested loop?

questions.q: Can an else block be used with a loop? When does it execute?
questions.short: Yes. The else block on a loop runs when the loop completes normally (without hitting break). It does NOT run if the loop was exited via break.
questions.detailed:
• For loop else: for x in lst: if condition: break else: print("not found").
  • While loop else: same pattern — else runs if the condition becomes False naturally.
  • Use case: searching — the else block runs only when the item was not found.
Key Point

Loop-else is a uniquely Pythonic feature. It eliminates the need for a found-flag variable in search loops.

Common Trap

The loop else does NOT mean "else if the loop did not run". It means "else if the loop was not broken out of". If the iterable is empty, the for loop body never runs, but the else DOES run.
followups: When does the else block of a loop NOT run?
Is loop-else common in real Python code?
What is the else block on a try-except?

questions.q: What are infinite loops and how do you avoid them?
questions.short: An infinite loop runs forever because its condition never becomes False or there is no break. Always ensure loops have a clear exit condition.
questions.detailed:
• while True: with a break inside is a valid and common pattern.
  • Accidental infinite loops: off-by-one errors, forgetting to increment a counter, modifying the wrong variable.
  • Prevention: ensure the loop variable changes with each iteration, or use a counter limit.
Key Point

In Python, while True: ... break is a common pattern for "do-while" behaviour (run at least once). It is not a bug — as long as there is a clear break condition inside.

Common Trap

Accidentally assigning inside a loop instead of comparing is a common cause: while x = 5: is a syntax error in Python (unlike C), so this particular mistake is caught. But logical errors like forgetting to update the counter are not.
followups: What is a do-while equivalent in Python?
How do you add a timeout to a while loop?
What is the while True pattern used for?

questions.q: What is the iterator protocol in Python?
questions.short: The iterator protocol requires an object to implement __iter__() (returns self) and __next__() (returns next value or raises StopIteration).
questions.detailed:
• __iter__(self): Called by iter(). Must return the iterator object itself.
  • __next__(self): Called by next(). Returns the next value. Raises StopIteration when exhausted.
  • Python's for loop calls these automatically: iter() once, then next() repeatedly.
Key Point

Any class implementing both __iter__ and __next__ is an iterator. Any class implementing only __iter__ (returning a separate iterator) is just an iterable.

Pro Tip

Generators implement the iterator protocol automatically. Using yield in a function turns it into a generator that handles __iter__ and __next__ for you.
followups: What is the difference between __iter__ and __next__?
What raises StopIteration?
How do generators relate to iterators?

questions.q: How do you create a custom iterator in Python?
questions.short: Create a custom iterator by defining a class with __iter__() and __next__() methods, or simply use a generator function with yield.
questions.detailed:
Class-based approach:
  • Define __iter__(self): return self.
  • Define __next__(self): compute next value and raise StopIteration when done.
Generator approach (simpler):
  • Write a function with yield. Python handles __iter__ and __next__ automatically.
  • def countdown(n): while n > 0: yield n; n -= 1.
Key Point

Generators are almost always easier than class-based iterators. Use a class only when you need to maintain complex state or add extra methods to the iterator.

Pro Tip

You can also use itertools module functions to create and combine iterators without writing any class or generator from scratch.
followups: What is a generator?
When would you use a class-based iterator vs a generator?
What is itertools?

questions.q: What does the next() function do?
questions.short: next(iterator) retrieves the next value from an iterator. When exhausted, it raises StopIteration. You can provide a default to avoid the exception.
questions.detailed:
• next(it): Returns the next value. Raises StopIteration when the iterator is exhausted.
  • next(it, default): Returns default instead of raising StopIteration.
  • Example: it = iter([1,2,3]); next(it) gives 1, then 2, then 3, then StopIteration.
Key Point

Providing a default to next() is the safe pattern: next(it, None) returns None when exhausted instead of crashing.

Common Trap

Once an iterator is exhausted, calling next() on it always raises StopIteration — even if you call iter() on it again (iterators return themselves from __iter__).
followups: How do you reset an iterator?
What is StopIteration?
Can you use next() on a list directly?

questions.q: When would you prefer a while loop over a for loop?
questions.short: Use while when the number of iterations is unknown, you need to loop until a condition changes, or you are implementing a do-while pattern.
questions.detailed:
Prefer while over for when:
  • Number of iterations depends on runtime data (user input, network response, file content).
  • You need a "do at least once" pattern: while True: ... if condition: break.
  • Iterating until a sentinel value is found.
  • Implementing a retry loop with a maximum attempt count.
Key Point

Most loops in Python should be for loops because iterating over collections is the most common case. Reserve while for condition-based repetition.

Pro Tip

A common while pattern: reading chunks from a file — while chunk := f.read(8192): using the walrus operator.
followups: What is a do-while equivalent in Python?
How do you limit a while loop to n iterations?
What is the walrus operator?

questions.q: What are user-defined functions and when should you use them?
questions.short: User-defined functions are reusable blocks of code defined with def. Use them to avoid repetition, improve readability, and separate concerns.
questions.detailed:
• Defined with def function_name(parameters):.
  • Called by name: function_name(arguments).
  • Can return a value with return, or return None implicitly.
  • Can be defined inside other functions, passed as arguments, or stored in variables.
When to use functions:
  • Any code you write more than once.
  • Logic that can be named and described clearly.
  • To separate concerns and make testing easier.
Key Point

A function should do one thing well. If you cannot describe a function in one sentence, it probably does too much.

Pro Tip

Follow the DRY principle: Do Not Repeat Yourself. If you copy-paste code, consider extracting it into a function.
followups: What is the difference between a function and a method?
What does a function return if it has no return statement?
What are first-class functions?

questions.q: Differentiate between arguments and parameters.
questions.short: Parameters are variables in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values passed when calling the function.
questions.detailed:
• Parameter: The variable name in the function signature. def fn(x, y): — x and y are parameters.
  • Argument: The value passed when calling. fn(3, 5) — 3 and 5 are arguments.
Types of arguments:
  • Positional: Matched by position. fn(3, 5).
  • Keyword: Matched by name. fn(y=5, x=3).
  • Default: Parameter has a fallback value. def fn(x, y=10).
  • *args: Captures extra positional args as a tuple.
  • **kwargs: Captures extra keyword args as a dict.
Key Point

Arguments are evaluated before being passed to the function. Python passes object references — not copies of values (except for immutable objects where mutation is impossible).

Common Trap

Default mutable argument is a classic bug: def fn(lst=[]):. The default list is created once and shared across all calls. Use def fn(lst=None): if lst is None: lst = [] instead.
followups: What is the mutable default argument problem?
What are *args and **kwargs?
What is the difference between keyword and positional arguments?

questions.q: What is the difference between print and return in a function?
questions.short: print() displays output to the console and returns None. return sends a value back to the caller and exits the function.
questions.detailed:
• print(x): Displays x. Side effect only. Returns None.
  • return x: Sends x back to the caller. Exits the function immediately.
  • A function with only print() cannot be used in expressions: result = fn() would give None.
  • A function with return can be chained: result = fn1(fn2(x)).
Key Point

Always use return when the function produces a value that the caller needs. Use print() only for display purposes — never as a substitute for return.

Common Trap

A common beginner mistake: def double(x): print(x * 2) and then result = double(5). This prints 10 but result is None. The function should return x * 2 instead.
followups: What does a function return if it has no return statement?
Can a function have both print and return?
What is a side effect?

questions.q: Explain *args and **kwargs in detail with examples.
questions.short: *args captures extra positional arguments as a tuple. **kwargs captures extra keyword arguments as a dict. Both allow functions to accept a variable number of arguments.
questions.detailed:
• def fn(*args): args is a tuple of all extra positional arguments.
  • def fn(**kwargs): kwargs is a dict of all extra keyword arguments.
  • def fn(*args, **kwargs): Accept any combination.
  • Order in signature: def fn(pos, *args, kw_only, **kwargs).
Unpacking on call:
  • fn(*[1,2,3]): Unpacks list as positional arguments.
  • fn(**{"a":1, "b":2}): Unpacks dict as keyword arguments.
Key Point

The names args and kwargs are convention, not requirement. The * and ** are the actual syntax. You can write *nums or **options.

Common Trap

Parameters after * are keyword-only: def fn(a, *, b): — b must be passed as fn(1, b=2). Forgetting this when calling raises TypeError.
followups: What is the order of parameters in a function signature?
What is argument unpacking?
What are keyword-only arguments?

questions.q: What are recursive functions? Explain with a use case.
questions.short: A recursive function calls itself. It needs a base case (stopping condition) and a recursive case. Without a base case it recurses infinitely and raises RecursionError.
questions.detailed:
• Base case: The condition under which the function stops calling itself.
  • Recursive case: The function calls itself with a simpler version of the problem.
  • Example — factorial: def factorial(n): return 1 if n <= 1 else n * factorial(n-1).
  • Python's default recursion limit is 1000 (sys.getrecursionlimit()).
Key Point

Every recursive algorithm can be rewritten iteratively. Recursion is elegant for tree/graph traversal, divide-and-conquer, and problems naturally defined recursively (factorial, Fibonacci, merge sort).

Common Trap

Without a base case, Python raises RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded. For large inputs, iterative solutions are safer and often faster due to function call overhead.
followups: What is a base case?
What is the recursion limit in Python?
How do you convert a recursive function to iterative?

questions.q: What are higher-order functions in Python?
questions.short: Higher-order functions take other functions as arguments or return functions as results. Examples: map(), filter(), sorted(), and any function that accepts a callback.
questions.detailed:
• Functions as arguments: sorted(lst, key=lambda x: x[1]) — key is a function argument.
  • Functions as return values: A factory function that returns a configured function.
  • Built-in examples: map(fn, iterable), filter(fn, iterable), functools.reduce(fn, iterable).
Key Point

Higher-order functions are fundamental to functional programming. In Python, functions are first-class objects — they can be stored in variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.

Pro Tip

Decorators are a form of higher-order functions — they take a function, wrap it, and return the wrapped version.
followups: What are first-class functions?
What is a decorator?
What is the difference between map() and a list comprehension?

questions.q: What are default arguments in Python?
questions.short: Default arguments provide a fallback value when the caller does not pass that argument. They are defined in the function signature with =.
questions.detailed:
• Syntax: def fn(x, y=10, z="hello"):.
  • If y is not passed, it defaults to 10.
  • Default parameters must come after non-default parameters.
  • Can be any expression, evaluated once at function definition time.
Key Point

Default values are evaluated ONCE when the function is defined, not each time it is called. This is fine for immutable defaults (int, str, None) but dangerous for mutable defaults (list, dict).

Common Trap

Never use a mutable object (list, dict) as a default: def fn(lst=[]):. The same list object is shared across all calls. Use def fn(lst=None): if lst is None: lst = [] instead.
followups: What is the mutable default argument problem?
Can default arguments be expressions?
What order must default arguments appear in?

questions.q: Differentiate between keyword arguments and positional arguments.
questions.short: Positional arguments are matched by position. Keyword arguments are matched by name and can be passed in any order.
questions.detailed:
• Positional: fn(1, 2, 3) — matched left to right.
  • Keyword: fn(z=3, x=1, y=2) — matched by name, any order.
  • Mixed: fn(1, z=3, y=2) — positional first, then keyword.
  • Positional-only (Python 3.8+): Parameters before / must be positional.
  • Keyword-only: Parameters after * must be keyword.
Key Point

Keyword arguments make function calls self-documenting. create_user("Alice", age=30, admin=True) is clearer than create_user("Alice", 30, True).

Common Trap

Positional arguments must always come before keyword arguments in a function call. fn(x=1, 2) raises SyntaxError: positional argument follows keyword argument.
followups: What are positional-only parameters?
What are keyword-only parameters?
What is the / and * in function signatures?

questions.q: What is the mutable default argument problem in Python?
questions.short: The mutable default argument bug occurs when a mutable object (list, dict) is used as a default parameter. It is created once and shared across all calls, accumulating state.
questions.detailed:
• def add_item(item, lst=[]): — the list is created ONCE at function definition.
  • First call: add_item(1) gives [1].
  • Second call: add_item(2) gives [1, 2] — NOT [2]!
  • The list persists between calls because it is the same object.
Fix: Use None as sentinel:
  • def add_item(item, lst=None):
  • if lst is None: lst = []
  • lst.append(item); return lst
Key Point

This is one of the most famous Python gotchas. The default value is evaluated when the function is defined, not when it is called.

Common Trap

This also applies to dicts: def fn(d={}): shares the same dict across all calls. Always use None and create a fresh mutable object inside the function.
followups: What is the fix for mutable default arguments?
Does this apply to all mutable types?
Why does Python evaluate defaults at definition time?

questions.q: What are first-class functions in Python?
questions.short: First-class functions means functions are objects. They can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and stored in data structures.
questions.detailed:
• Assign to variable: greet = print; greet("hello").
  • Pass as argument: sorted(lst, key=len) — len is a function passed as argument.
  • Return from function: A factory function returns a customised function.
  • Store in list/dict: [int, str, float] — list of type constructors.
Key Point

First-class functions are what makes decorators, callbacks, and higher-order functions possible in Python. Everything is an object — including functions.

Pro Tip

You can check a function's name with fn.__name__ and its docstring with fn.__doc__. This metadata is useful in decorators.
followups: What are higher-order functions?
What is a callback?
What is a decorator?

questions.q: What are closures in Python and how do they work?
questions.short: A closure is a nested function that captures and remembers variables from its enclosing scope, even after the outer function has returned.
questions.detailed:
• The inner function has access to the enclosing scope's variables.
  • These variables are kept alive even after the outer function returns.
  • Example: a multiplier factory — def make_multiplier(n): def multiply(x): return x * n; return multiply.
  • double = make_multiplier(2); double(5) gives 10 — n=2 is remembered.
Key Point

Closures enable function factories, decorators, and encapsulation of state without using a class. They are one of the most powerful features in Python.

Common Trap

Classic closure bug in loops: [lambda x: x+i for i in range(3)] — all lambdas share the same i (its final value). Fix: [lambda x, i=i: x+i for i in range(3)] to capture each i at creation time.
followups: What is the loop closure bug?
How is a closure different from a regular nested function?
What is the nonlocal keyword used for in closures?

questions.q: What are function annotations in Python?
questions.short: Function annotations attach metadata (usually type hints) to parameters and return values. They are not enforced at runtime but improve readability and enable static type checkers.
questions.detailed:
• Syntax: def fn(x: int, y: str = "hello") -> bool:.
  • Annotations are stored in fn.__annotations__.
  • Not enforced by Python at runtime — purely informational.
  • Type checkers like mypy and pyright use them to catch type errors before runtime.
  • Can be any expression: x: list[int], x: "forward reference".
Key Point

Annotations improve code documentation and IDE support (autocompletion, type checking). They are especially valuable in large codebases and team projects.

Pro Tip

Use from __future__ import annotations (Python 3.10+) or from typing import Optional, List, Dict for more complex type hints.
followups: What are type hints?
What is mypy?
Are annotations enforced at runtime?

questions.q: Compare recursion vs iterative loops — when to use each.
questions.short: Use recursion for naturally recursive problems (trees, graphs, divide-and-conquer). Use iteration when performance matters or depth could exceed Python's recursion limit.
questions.detailed:
• Recursion strengths: Clean and expressive for tree traversal, Fibonacci, merge sort, permutations.
  • Recursion weaknesses: Overhead of function calls, Python's 1000-call default limit, no tail-call optimisation in Python.
  • Iteration strengths: More memory-efficient, no recursion depth limit, generally faster.
  • Iteration weaknesses: Can be less readable for inherently recursive problems.
Key Point

Python does not optimise tail recursion. A recursive Fibonacci with large n will hit RecursionError. Use iterative or memoised versions for production code.

Pro Tip

Use @functools.lru_cache to add memoisation to recursive functions, dramatically improving performance for overlapping subproblems.
followups: What is tail recursion?
What is memoisation?
What is the recursion limit in Python?

questions.q: What is the partial() function from functools?
questions.short: partial() from functools creates a new function with some arguments of another function pre-filled. It is useful for creating specialised versions of generic functions.
questions.detailed:
• from functools import partial.
  • double = partial(pow, exp=2) — pre-fills exp=2.
  • double(5) gives 25.
  • add_5 = partial(lambda x, y: x + y, 5) — first arg pre-filled.
  • Useful for callbacks that need extra parameters.
Key Point

partial() is cleaner than writing a new lambda just to fix some arguments. It also preserves the original function's metadata (__name__, __doc__).

Pro Tip

Use partial when integrating with APIs that expect a function with a specific signature but you want to pre-configure some arguments.
followups: What is currying?
How is partial() different from a lambda?
What is functools?

questions.q: Differentiate between methods and functions in Python.
questions.short: Functions are standalone blocks of code defined with def. Methods are functions defined inside a class and always take self (or cls) as their first parameter.
questions.detailed:
• Function: Defined at module level. Called as fn(args).
  • Method: Defined inside a class. Called as obj.method(args). Python automatically passes the instance as the first argument.
  • Instance method: Takes self — has access to instance data.
  • Class method: Takes cls, decorated with @classmethod.
  • Static method: Takes neither, decorated with @staticmethod.
Key Point

Every method is technically a function. The distinction is that methods are bound to an object and receive it automatically as the first argument.

Common Trap

Calling a method without parentheses gives you the method object, not the result: obj.method is the function object; obj.method() calls it.
followups: What is self?
What is the difference between classmethod and staticmethod?
Can you call a method without an instance?

questions.q: What is a lambda function and how does it differ from a regular function?
questions.short: A lambda is an anonymous, single-expression function. Unlike def, it cannot contain statements, multiple expressions, or have a docstring.
questions.detailed:
• Syntax: lambda parameters: expression.
  • square = lambda x: x**2 is equivalent to def square(x): return x**2.
  • Can take multiple arguments: lambda x, y: x + y.
  • Commonly used as inline key functions: sorted(lst, key=lambda x: x[1]).
Key Point

Lambdas are best used for short, throwaway functions — especially as arguments to map(), filter(), sorted(), or any function expecting a callable.

Common Trap

PEP 8 discourages assigning lambdas to names: square = lambda x: x**2. Use def square(x): return x**2 instead — it is clearer, debuggable, and has a proper __name__.
followups: What is the difference between lambda and def?
When should you use lambda vs def?
Can lambda have multiple expressions?

questions.q: Explain map(), filter(), and reduce() with examples.
questions.short: map() applies a function to each element of an iterable. filter() keeps elements where the function returns True. reduce() applies a function cumulatively to produce a single value.
questions.detailed:
• map(fn, iterable): Returns an iterator of fn(x) for each x. list(map(str, [1,2,3])) gives ["1","2","3"].
  • filter(fn, iterable): Returns an iterator of x where fn(x) is True. list(filter(None, [0,1,2,False])) gives [1,2].
  • from functools import reduce; reduce(lambda a,b: a+b, [1,2,3,4]) gives 10.
Key Point

In modern Python, list comprehensions and generator expressions are preferred over map() and filter() for readability. But map() and filter() return lazy iterators, which can be more memory-efficient.

Pro Tip

reduce() is not a built-in in Python 3 — it was moved to functools. Always import it: from functools import reduce.
followups: Is map() lazy in Python 3?
How is map() different from a list comprehension?
Where is reduce() in Python 3?

questions.q: What is functional programming and does Python support it?
questions.short: Functional programming treats computation as the evaluation of mathematical functions, avoiding shared state and mutation. Python supports it with first-class functions, lambda, map, filter, and functools.
questions.detailed:
• Core concepts: pure functions (no side effects), immutability, higher-order functions, function composition.
  • Python support: first-class functions, lambda, map(), filter(), reduce(), functools, closures.
  • Pure function: Returns the same output for the same input, no side effects.
  • Python is multi-paradigm — it supports OOP, procedural, and functional styles.
Key Point

Python is not a purely functional language (it has mutable state and loops), but it provides enough functional tools to write clean, functional-style code when appropriate.

Pro Tip

Functional programming makes code easier to test and reason about. Pure functions have no hidden state — you can test them in isolation.
followups: What is a pure function?
What is immutability in functional programming?
What is functools?

questions.q: What are anonymous functions in Python?
questions.short: Anonymous functions are functions without a name. In Python, they are created with the lambda keyword. They are single-expression functions used inline.
questions.detailed:
• Created with lambda: lambda x: x * 2.
  • Have no name (__name__ is "").
  • Cannot be reused unless assigned to a variable (but PEP 8 discourages that).
  • Ideal as short-lived callbacks: sorted(people, key=lambda p: p["age"]).
Key Point

The main advantage of anonymous functions is brevity — you do not need to define a full function when you only need it in one place for one purpose.

Common Trap

Lambdas cannot contain statements (no if-else blocks, no assignments, no loops). They can use the ternary expression: lambda x: "even" if x%2==0 else "odd".
followups: What is the difference between lambda and def?
Can lambda have conditions?
What is __name__ for a lambda?

questions.q: What is a list comprehension and when should you use it?
questions.short: A list comprehension is a concise way to create a list: [expression for item in iterable if condition]. It is faster and more readable than an equivalent for loop.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: [x**2 for x in range(10)].
  • With filter: [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0].
  • With function: [fn(x) for x in lst].
  • Nested: [x for row in matrix for x in row] (flatten).
Key Point

List comprehensions are faster than for loops in CPython because the loop is executed in C internals. They also produce a list in one expression, making intent clear.

Common Trap

List comprehensions always build the entire list in memory. If you only need to iterate once, use a generator expression to avoid memory overhead: (x**2 for x in range(1000000)) instead of [x**2 for x in range(1000000)].
followups: What is a generator expression?
Are list comprehensions faster than for loops?
Can you have nested list comprehensions?

questions.q: What is a dictionary comprehension?
questions.short: A dictionary comprehension creates a dict: {key: value for item in iterable if condition}. Useful for transforming, filtering, or inverting dicts.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: {word: len(word) for word in words}.
  • Invert: {v: k for k, v in d.items()}.
  • Filter: {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > 0}.
  • Transform: {k: v.upper() for k, v in d.items()}.
Key Point

Dict comprehensions are the most Pythonic way to create a transformed or filtered version of an existing dict.

Common Trap

If the inversion {v: k for k, v in d.items()} has duplicate values, only the last key survives. Ensure your original dict has unique values before inverting.
followups: How do you invert a dictionary?
What is the difference between dict() and a dict comprehension?
Can you have conditions in a dict comprehension?

questions.q: What is a set comprehension?
questions.short: A set comprehension creates a set: {expression for item in iterable if condition}. Results are automatically deduplicated.
questions.detailed:
• Basic: {x**2 for x in range(10)} — set of squares, no duplicates.
  • Filter: {x for x in lst if x > 0} — unique positive values.
  • Unique lengths: {len(w) for w in words}.
Key Point

Set comprehensions are perfect when you want the unique results of some operation — you get deduplication for free.

Common Trap

The syntax {expr for item in iterable} is a set comprehension. The syntax {key: val for item in iterable} is a dict comprehension. Do not confuse them — the colon makes the difference.
followups: How is a set comprehension different from a list comprehension?
What is a frozenset comprehension?
Can you create a frozenset with a comprehension?

questions.q: What is a generator expression and how is it different from a list comprehension?
questions.short: A generator expression is like a list comprehension but lazy — it produces values one at a time on demand instead of building the whole list in memory.
questions.detailed:
• Syntax: (expression for item in iterable if condition) — parentheses instead of brackets.
  • sum(x**2 for x in range(1000000)): Never builds the full list — saves huge memory.
  • Can only be iterated once — not reusable like a list.
  • Useful when feeding into functions: max(len(w) for w in words).
Key Point

When passing a generator expression as the only argument to a function, you can omit the extra parentheses: sum(x**2 for x in range(10)) instead of sum((x**2 for x in range(10))).

Common Trap

Generator expressions are exhausted after one iteration. If you need to iterate multiple times, use a list comprehension. g = (x for x in range(5)); list(g); list(g) — the second list() gives [].
followups: What is the difference between a generator expression and a list comprehension?
When would you prefer a generator expression?
Can you reuse a generator?

questions.q: How do nested comprehensions work in Python?
questions.short: Nested comprehensions have multiple for clauses. The first for is the outer loop, the second is the inner loop. They read left to right, matching loop nesting order.
questions.detailed:
• Flatten a matrix: [x for row in matrix for x in row].
  • All pairs: [(x,y) for x in range(3) for y in range(3)].
  • With condition: [x for row in matrix for x in row if x > 0].
Reading rule: the leftmost for is the outermost loop. Equivalent to:
  • result = []
  • for row in matrix:
  • for x in row:
  • result.append(x)
Key Point

The order of for clauses in a nested comprehension matches the order of nested for loops in equivalent imperative code.

Common Trap

Nested comprehensions deeper than two levels become very hard to read. If you need three or more levels of nesting, use regular for loops with descriptive variable names.
followups: How do you flatten a 2D list with a comprehension?
Can you mix list and dict comprehensions?
When should you avoid nested comprehensions?

questions.q: Explain classes and objects in Python.
questions.short: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. An object is an instance of a class with its own data (attributes) and behaviour (methods).
questions.detailed:
• Define a class: class Dog:.
  • Create an object (instantiate): my_dog = Dog().
  • Attributes: Data stored on the object. my_dog.name = "Rex".
  • Methods: Functions defined in the class. my_dog.bark().
  • Each object has its own copy of instance attributes but shares class methods.
Key Point

In Python, everything is an object — ints, strings, functions, even classes themselves. Use type(obj) to see what class an object belongs to.

Pro Tip

Use isinstance(obj, ClassName) to check if an object is an instance of a class. It also handles subclasses correctly.
followups: What is the difference between a class and an object?
What is instantiation?
What is __init__?

questions.q: What are the four pillars of OOP? Explain each.
questions.short: The four pillars of OOP are Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism (EAIP).
questions.detailed:
• Encapsulation: Bundling data (attributes) and behaviour (methods) into one unit (class). Hiding internal state from direct access.
  • Abstraction: Exposing only the necessary interface and hiding implementation details. Users know WHAT an object does, not HOW.
  • Inheritance: A child class inherits attributes and methods from a parent class. Enables code reuse.
  • Polymorphism: Different classes can be used through the same interface. The same method call behaves differently depending on the object type.
Key Point

A helpful way to remember: Encapsulation = hide data, Abstraction = hide complexity, Inheritance = reuse code, Polymorphism = same interface, different behaviour.

Common Trap

Encapsulation and Abstraction are often confused. Encapsulation is about data hiding using access control. Abstraction is about simplifying complexity by exposing only what is needed.
followups: What is the difference between encapsulation and abstraction?
How is polymorphism implemented in Python?
What is multiple inheritance?

questions.q: What is a constructor in Python? Explain __init__ and __new__.
questions.short: __init__ is the initialiser called after object creation. __new__ creates the object. In practice, you override __init__ to set up instance attributes.
questions.detailed:
• __new__(cls): Called first. Allocates memory and creates the object. Returns the new instance. Rarely overridden.
  • __init__(self, ...): Called immediately after __new__. Initialises the object's attributes. Does NOT create the object.
  • When you write obj = MyClass(), Python calls MyClass.__new__(MyClass) then MyClass.__init__(obj).
Key Point

__init__ is NOT the constructor — it is the initialiser. The constructor is __new__. But in practice, __init__ is where you set up your object, so it is colloquially called the constructor.

Common Trap

__init__ should never return a value (other than None). Returning a non-None value from __init__ raises TypeError.
followups: What is the difference between __init__ and __new__?
Can __init__ return a value?
When would you override __new__?

questions.q: What are destructors in Python? Explain the __del__ method.
questions.short: __del__ is the destructor method called when an object is about to be garbage collected. It is rarely used and not guaranteed to run.
questions.detailed:
• Defined as: def __del__(self):.
  • Called by the garbage collector when the object's reference count drops to 0.
  • NOT guaranteed to run at a specific time, or at all (e.g. if the interpreter exits).
  • Do not use it for resource cleanup — use context managers (with) instead.
Key Point

For file handles, database connections, and other resources, always use context managers (with statement) for cleanup. Do NOT rely on __del__ for critical cleanup.

Common Trap

__del__ is not the same as deleting a variable with del. del x removes the reference. __del__ is called when ALL references to the object are gone.
followups: What is a context manager?
What is the with statement?
What is garbage collection?

questions.q: What is the purpose of the self keyword in Python?
questions.short: self is a reference to the current instance of the class. It is the first parameter of every instance method, passed automatically by Python when you call a method on an object.
questions.detailed:
• self is just a convention — you can name it anything, but self is universal.
  • When you call obj.method(arg), Python translates it to ClassName.method(obj, arg).
  • Through self, the method can access instance attributes: self.name.
  • Through self, the method can call other methods: self.other_method().
Key Point

self is not a keyword in Python — it is a naming convention. The first parameter of an instance method receives the instance automatically. You could write def method(this): and it would work, but never do this.

Common Trap

Forgetting self when defining an instance method is a common mistake. def method(): instead of def method(self): raises TypeError when called.
followups: Is self a keyword in Python?
What is the difference between self and cls?
Why does Python require explicit self?

questions.q: Explain inheritance in Python and its types.
questions.short: Inheritance allows a child class to inherit attributes and methods from a parent class. Python supports single, multiple, and multilevel inheritance.
questions.detailed:
• Syntax: class Dog(Animal): — Dog inherits from Animal.
  • Single: One parent class.
  • Multiple: class C(A, B): — inherits from A and B. Python uses MRO to resolve conflicts.
  • Multilevel: A inherits B, B inherits C.
  • Hierarchical: Multiple classes inherit from the same parent.
Key Point

Python uses C3 linearisation (MRO) to determine method resolution order in multiple inheritance. Use ClassName.__mro__ or help(ClassName) to see the MRO.

Pro Tip

Use super() to call parent class methods from a child class. It respects MRO and is the correct way to extend parent behaviour.
followups: What is MRO?
What is super()?
What is the diamond problem?

questions.q: What is the super() keyword and when is it used?
questions.short: super() returns a proxy object that delegates method calls to the parent class. It respects the MRO and is used to call overridden methods from a child class.
questions.detailed:
• super().__init__(args): Call parent class initialiser from child.
  • super().method(): Call parent class method from child.
  • In Python 3, super() with no arguments works inside class methods.
  • Respects MRO — ensures correct order in multiple inheritance.
Key Point

Always call super().__init__() in a child class __init__ to ensure the parent class is properly initialised.

Common Trap

Calling the parent class directly (Animal.__init__(self)) bypasses MRO. In multiple inheritance, this can skip some initialisers. Always use super() instead.
followups: What is MRO?
When must you call super().__init__()?
What happens if you don't call super().__init__?

questions.q: What is method overriding?
questions.short: Method overriding is when a child class provides its own implementation of a method that already exists in the parent class.
questions.detailed:
• Define a method in the child class with the same name as in the parent.
  • When the method is called on a child instance, Python uses the child's version.
  • Call the parent's version with super().method().
  • Enables polymorphism — different classes responding differently to the same method call.
Key Point

Method overriding is the mechanism behind polymorphism. It lets you customise inherited behaviour without modifying the parent class.

Common Trap

Method overriding is different from method overloading. Python does not support traditional method overloading (same name, different signatures). The last defined method wins.
followups: What is the difference between overriding and overloading?
How do you call the parent method from an override?
What is polymorphism?

questions.q: Can Python perform method overloading? Explain.
questions.short: Python does not support traditional method overloading. If you define the same method twice, the second definition replaces the first. Use default arguments or *args to simulate it.
questions.detailed:
• In languages like Java, overloading means multiple methods with the same name but different signatures.
  • Python only keeps the last definition of a method with a given name.
  • Simulation with defaults: def fn(x, y=None): if y is None: ....
  • Simulation with *args: def fn(*args): if len(args) == 1: ....
  • Python 3.11+ has functools.singledispatch for type-based dispatch.
Key Point

Python's flexible default arguments and *args/kwargs make traditional overloading unnecessary. One function can handle multiple call signatures.

Common Trap

Defining the same method twice in a class is a silent bug — the second silently replaces the first. Python raises no warning.
followups: What is singledispatch?
What is the difference between overloading and overriding?
How do you handle multiple method signatures in Python?

questions.q: Explain polymorphism and its types in Python.
questions.short: Polymorphism means different objects can respond to the same interface (method call) in their own way. Python implements it via duck typing and method overriding.
questions.detailed:
• Method overriding: Child class overrides parent method. animal.speak() calls Dog's speak() for a Dog object and Cat's speak() for a Cat object.
  • Duck typing: If an object has the right methods, it can be used regardless of its class.
  • Operator overloading: Same operator (+) behaves differently on int, str, list.
  • Ad-hoc polymorphism: Functions like len() work on strings, lists, tuples, dicts.
Key Point

Python's duck typing is a form of polymorphism: if it has a .quack() method, treat it as a duck — no need for a common base class.

Pro Tip

Python's built-in functions like len(), str(), iter() are polymorphic — they work differently based on the type of object passed.
followups: What is duck typing?
What is the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism?
How does Python implement polymorphism?

questions.q: What is encapsulation and how is it implemented in Python?
questions.short: Encapsulation bundles data and methods into a class, and restricts direct access to internal state. Python uses naming conventions (_private, __mangled) instead of strict access control.
questions.detailed:
• Public: No prefix. Accessible from anywhere. self.name.
  • Protected (convention): Single underscore self._name. Signals internal use — accessible but "do not touch".
  • Private (name mangling): Double underscore self.__name. Python renames to _ClassName__name to prevent accidental access.
Key Point

Python does not enforce access control like Java. Single and double underscores are conventions that indicate intent. The double underscore triggers name mangling — not true privacy.

Common Trap

self.__name is not truly private. It is renamed to self._ClassName__name and can still be accessed. The mangling is to avoid name clashes in inheritance, not to enforce privacy.
followups: What is name mangling?
What is the difference between _ and __ prefix?
How do you implement true privacy in Python?

questions.q: What is abstraction and how is it implemented in Python?
questions.short: Abstraction hides implementation complexity, exposing only the necessary interface. In Python, it is implemented using abstract base classes (ABC) and abstract methods.
questions.detailed:
• from abc import ABC, abstractmethod.
  • Create abstract class: class Shape(ABC):.
  • Define abstract method: @abstractmethod def area(self): pass.
  • Subclasses MUST implement all abstract methods or they cannot be instantiated.
  • Python raises TypeError if you try to instantiate an abstract class.
Key Point

Abstract classes define a contract — they specify what methods subclasses must implement without providing the implementation. This enforces a consistent interface.

Common Trap

A class with @abstractmethod cannot be instantiated directly. If a subclass does not implement all abstract methods, it also cannot be instantiated.
followups: What is ABC in Python?
What is an abstract method?
What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class?

questions.q: Differentiate between abstraction and encapsulation.
questions.short: Encapsulation is about restricting access to internal data. Abstraction is about hiding complexity and exposing a clean interface. They are complementary, not the same.
questions.detailed:
• Encapsulation: HOW data is stored and protected. Bundles data + methods, uses _ and __ to signal access levels.
  • Abstraction: WHAT the object does, not how. Hides implementation details. Exposes only necessary methods.
  • Example: A Car class uses encapsulation to hide the engine state (self.__engine). It uses abstraction by exposing start() and stop() without revealing engine internals.
Key Point

Both work together: encapsulation hides the data, abstraction hides the complexity. Together they produce clean, maintainable, testable code.

Pro Tip

Think of a TV remote: abstraction = you only see buttons (what it does). Encapsulation = the circuit board is hidden inside the remote (how it works).
followups: What is the difference between encapsulation and data hiding?
How is abstraction implemented in Python?
What is an interface?

questions.q: Differentiate between class variables and instance variables.
questions.short: Class variables are shared across all instances. Instance variables belong to a specific object. Assignment to self.x creates an instance variable; assignment to ClassName.x changes the class variable.
questions.detailed:
• Class variable: Defined in the class body, outside methods. Shared by all instances.
  • Instance variable: Defined inside methods using self. Each instance has its own copy.
  • Reading: if an instance variable exists, it shadows the class variable for that instance.
  • Writing: obj.x = 10 creates/updates an instance variable. ClassName.x = 10 changes the class variable for all instances.
Key Point

Class variables are good for shared state or constants. Instance variables are for per-object data. Mixing them up is a common source of bugs.

Common Trap

If a class variable is mutable (list, dict), it is shared across ALL instances. Modifying it via one instance affects all. Create instance variables (via self) to avoid this.
followups: What is the difference between class and instance attributes?
How do you access class variables from an instance?
What happens when you set self.x when x is a class variable?

questions.q: Compare @classmethod, @staticmethod, and instance methods.
questions.short: Instance methods take self. Class methods take cls and are decorated with @classmethod. Static methods take neither and are decorated with @staticmethod.
questions.detailed:
• Instance method: def method(self):. Has access to the instance and class. Most common.
  • Class method: @classmethod def method(cls):. Has access to the class but not the specific instance. Used for factory methods.
  • Static method: @staticmethod def method():. No access to instance or class. A plain utility function namespaced inside the class.
Key Point

Use @classmethod when the method needs access to class-level data or should create an instance. Use @staticmethod when the method logically belongs to the class but does not need instance or class access.

Common Trap

Calling @classmethod on an instance still passes the CLASS as cls, not the instance. Calling @staticmethod on an instance works the same as calling it on the class.
followups: When would you use a classmethod?
When would you use a staticmethod?
What is a factory method?

questions.q: What are dunder (magic) methods in Python?
questions.short: Dunder (double underscore) methods are special methods with __name__ format that Python calls automatically in specific contexts. Examples: __init__, __str__, __len__, __add__.
questions.detailed:
• __init__: Called on object creation.
  • __str__: Called by str() and print(). User-friendly string.
  • __repr__: Called by repr(). Developer-facing string. Should be unambiguous.
  • __len__: Called by len().
  • __eq__: Called by ==.
  • __lt__, __gt__: Called by <, >.
  • __add__: Called by +.
  • __iter__, __next__: Make the object iterable.
Key Point

Implementing dunder methods makes your objects behave like Python built-ins. A class with __len__ and __getitem__ works with len(), indexing, and for loops.

Pro Tip

Always implement both __str__ and __repr__. If only __repr__ is defined, Python uses it for both. If only __str__ is defined, repr() falls back to a default.
followups: What is the difference between __str__ and __repr__?
What is operator overloading?
What dunder methods make a class iterable?

questions.q: What is Method Resolution Order (MRO) in Python?
questions.short: MRO (Method Resolution Order) defines the order Python searches for methods in class hierarchies, especially in multiple inheritance. Python uses C3 linearisation.
questions.detailed:
• View MRO: ClassName.__mro__ or ClassName.mro().
  • Python searches: current class, then parent classes left-to-right, depth-first, then object.
  • C3 linearisation ensures: local precedence order is respected, monotonicity is maintained.
  • The diamond problem: class D inherits B and C, both inherit A. MRO ensures A's method is only called once.
Key Point

The MRO is why super() works correctly in multiple inheritance. It does not just call the direct parent — it follows the MRO chain.

Common Trap

In multiple inheritance, the order of parent classes matters: class C(A, B) gives A priority over B. Swapping to class C(B, A) changes the MRO.
followups: What is the diamond problem?
What is C3 linearisation?
How does super() relate to MRO?

questions.q: Explain association, aggregation, and composition in OOP.
questions.short: Association is a general relationship between classes. Aggregation is a "has-a" relationship where both can exist independently. Composition is a stronger "has-a" where the part cannot exist without the whole.
questions.detailed:
• Association: Classes use each other but are independent. A Teacher and a Student are associated.
  • Aggregation: One class contains another, but both can exist independently. A Department has Professors — professors can exist without the department.
  • Composition: One class owns another — the part cannot exist without the whole. A House has Rooms — rooms cannot exist without the house.
Key Point

Composition is the strongest relationship (tight coupling). Aggregation is looser. Association is the loosest. In practice, prefer composition over inheritance for flexibility.

Pro Tip

"Favour composition over inheritance" is a well-known design principle. Composing objects from smaller parts is often more flexible and testable than deep inheritance hierarchies.
followups: What is the difference between aggregation and composition?
What does 'favour composition over inheritance' mean?
How do you implement composition in Python?

questions.q: What is name mangling in Python?
questions.short: Name mangling changes __attr to _ClassName__attr inside a class, preventing accidental overriding in subclasses. It is not true privacy.
questions.detailed:
• Any attribute with two leading underscores (__x) is renamed to _ClassName__x at compile time.
  • Purpose: avoid name clashes in inherited classes, not to enforce privacy.
  • self.__name in class Foo becomes self._Foo__name internally.
  • Still accessible externally: obj._Foo__name works.
Key Point

Name mangling is about preventing accidental override in subclasses, not enforcing privacy. If a subclass also defines __x, it becomes _SubClass__x, not conflicting with the parent's _Parent__x.

Common Trap

Single underscore _x is just a convention (do not use externally). Double underscore __x triggers mangling. Neither is truly private — Python has no access modifiers.
followups: What is the difference between _ and __ prefix?
Is name mangling true privacy?
How do you access a name-mangled attribute externally?

questions.q: What are access modifiers in Python?
questions.short: Python does not have true access modifiers. It uses naming conventions: public (name), protected (_name), private (__name with mangling). These are hints, not enforcement.
questions.detailed:
• Public: No prefix. Accessible from anywhere.
  • Protected (_name): By convention, for internal use. Still accessible externally. Signals "do not use unless you know what you are doing".
  • Private (__name): Name mangling applied. Renamed to _ClassName__name. Still accessible but awkward.
Key Point

Python trusts developers. The philosophy is "we are all consenting adults here". Conventions signal intent without enforcing restrictions.

Common Trap

Double underscore does NOT make an attribute truly private. It just changes the name. obj._ClassName__attr still accesses it. Do not rely on it for security.
followups: What is name mangling?
What is the difference between _ and __ prefix?
How does Python compare to Java in terms of access control?

questions.q: What is duck typing in Python?
questions.short: Duck typing means Python cares about what an object can DO, not what it IS. If it has the right methods, it can be used — regardless of its class.
questions.detailed:
• Origin: "If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck."
  • Python does not check types at compile time. It calls methods and handles AttributeError if absent.
  • A function that calls obj.area() works on any object with an area() method — Circle, Square, Triangle.
  • No need for interfaces or inheritance — just the right methods.
Key Point

Duck typing is what makes Python so flexible. Functions written to work with a "file-like object" work with real files, StringIO, and BytesIO because they all have read() and write().

Pro Tip

Use hasattr(obj, "method_name") or try/except AttributeError for safer duck typing checks instead of isinstance().
followups: What is the difference between duck typing and static typing?
What is EAFP in Python?
How does duck typing enable polymorphism?

questions.q: Explain pass-by-reference vs pass-by-value in Python.
questions.short: Python uses pass-by-object-reference (also called pass-by-assignment). The function receives a reference to the same object. Mutation affects the original; reassignment does not.
questions.detailed:
• For mutable objects (list, dict): The function receives a reference to the same object. Mutating it (lst.append(x)) changes the original.
  • For immutable objects (int, str, tuple): You cannot mutate them. Reassignment (x = 10) just rebinds the local name — the original is unchanged.
  • It is neither pure pass-by-value nor pure pass-by-reference.
Key Point

Python passes the reference itself by value. You can mutate the object the reference points to, but you cannot change what the reference points to (rebinding inside a function does not affect the caller).

Common Trap

A common interview question: "Is Python pass-by-value or pass-by-reference?" The correct answer is neither — it is pass-by-object-reference. Mutable objects appear pass-by-reference; immutables appear pass-by-value.
followups: What is the difference between mutation and rebinding?
How do you prevent a function from modifying a list you pass to it?
What is pass-by-value vs pass-by-reference?

questions.q: What are dataclasses in Python?
questions.short: Dataclasses (Python 3.7+) are classes decorated with @dataclass that auto-generate __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ from field definitions.
questions.detailed:
• from dataclasses import dataclass.
  • Decorate your class with @dataclass.
  • Define fields as class-level annotations: name: str; age: int = 0.
  • Python auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__.
  • Optional: frozen=True makes the dataclass immutable (like a tuple).
  • Optional: order=True auto-generates comparison methods.
Key Point

Dataclasses reduce boilerplate significantly. Instead of writing __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ manually, one decorator and type annotations do it all.

Pro Tip

For fully immutable, memory-efficient data containers, compare @dataclass(frozen=True) vs namedtuple. Frozen dataclasses are more modern and support inheritance better.
followups: What is the difference between dataclass and namedtuple?
What does frozen=True do in a dataclass?
What methods does @dataclass auto-generate?

questions.q: Explain the different file opening modes in Python.
questions.short: Python file modes: r (read), w (write/overwrite), a (append), x (create new), r+ (read+write). Add b for binary mode.
questions.detailed:
• 'r': Read. File must exist. Default mode.
  • 'w': Write. Creates file if absent. OVERWRITES if it exists.
  • 'a': Append. Creates if absent. Adds to end without overwriting.
  • 'x': Exclusive create. Fails if file already exists.
  • 'r+': Read and write. File must exist.
  • 'w+': Read and write. Creates or overwrites.
  • Add 'b' for binary: 'rb', 'wb'.
Key Point

Always use 'a' to add to a file without losing existing content. 'w' silently erases everything in the file.

Common Trap

Opening a file with 'w' immediately truncates it to zero bytes — even before you write anything. If you just want to check if a file exists, do not open it with 'w'.
followups: What is the difference between w and a mode?
What does r+ do?
What is binary mode?

questions.q: What happens when you open a non-existent file in different modes?
questions.short: Opening a non-existent file with r raises FileNotFoundError. With w or a, Python creates the file. With x, it also creates but raises FileExistsError if it already exists.
questions.detailed:
• open('file.txt', 'r'): Raises FileNotFoundError if file does not exist.
  • open('file.txt', 'w'): Creates the file if absent. Overwrites if present.
  • open('file.txt', 'a'): Creates the file if absent. Appends if present.
  • open('file.txt', 'x'): Creates new file. Raises FileExistsError if file already exists.
Key Point

Check if a file exists before opening it for reading: import os; os.path.exists('file.txt') or use pathlib.Path("file.txt").exists().

Common Trap

Opening with 'w' does not raise an error if the file does not exist — it creates it. But it silently overwrites any existing file. Use 'x' if you want to ensure you are creating a new file.
followups: How do you check if a file exists before opening?
What is FileNotFoundError?
What is the difference between w and x mode?

questions.q: Differentiate between read(), readline(), and readlines().
questions.short: read() returns the entire file as a string. readline() returns one line. readlines() returns a list of all lines.
questions.detailed:
• f.read(): Reads entire file content as one string. f.read(n) reads at most n bytes.
  • f.readline(): Reads one line including the newline character. Returns empty string at EOF.
  • f.readlines(): Reads all lines and returns a list. Each element includes the newline.
Most memory-efficient way to read:
  • for line in f:: Iterates line by line without loading everything into memory.
Key Point

For large files, iterate with for line in f:. It reads lazily — one line at a time — and does not load the whole file into memory.

Common Trap

After calling f.read(), the file cursor is at the end. Calling f.read() again returns an empty string. Use f.seek(0) to reset the cursor.
followups: How do you read a large file efficiently?
What does f.read() return after the file is exhausted?
What is the difference between readline() and readlines()?

questions.q: What are seek() and tell() used for in file handling?
questions.short: seek(offset) moves the file cursor to a specific position. tell() returns the current position of the cursor as a byte offset from the start.
questions.detailed:
• f.tell(): Returns current cursor position (bytes from start of file).
  • f.seek(0): Move cursor to start of file.
  • f.seek(0, 2): Move to end of file (2 means from end).
  • f.seek(n): Move cursor to byte position n.
  • Used to re-read a file after reading it once, or to read specific sections.
Key Point

After f.read() the cursor is at the end. Use f.seek(0) to reset it before reading again. This is more efficient than closing and reopening the file.

Pro Tip

The second argument to seek(): 0 = from start (default), 1 = from current position, 2 = from end. f.seek(0, 2) is the standard way to jump to the end.
followups: What does seek(0) do?
How do you find the file size using seek and tell?
Does seek work on all file types?

questions.q: What is the with statement and why is it preferred for file handling?
questions.short: The with statement (context manager) ensures the file is automatically closed when the block exits — even if an exception occurs.
questions.detailed:
• with open('file.txt') as f:: f is automatically closed when the block ends.
