  • No need to call f.close() manually.
  • Works even if an exception is raised inside the block.
  • Opens and closes the file in one clean block.
Key Point

Always use with open(...) for file handling. Forgetting to call close() can leak file descriptors, leading to data corruption or resource exhaustion.

Common Trap

If you open a file without with and an exception occurs before f.close(), the file stays open. The with statement guarantees cleanup via __exit__.
followups: What is a context manager?
What dunder methods does with use?
What happens if an exception occurs inside a with block?

questions.q: What is a context manager in Python?
questions.short: A context manager manages resources automatically with the with statement. It sets up a resource on entry (__enter__) and tears it down on exit (__exit__).
questions.detailed:
• Implemented with __enter__(self) and __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb).
  • The with statement calls __enter__ at the start and __exit__ at the end.
  • __exit__ is called even if an exception occurs — perfect for cleanup.
  • Can also be created using @contextmanager decorator from contextlib.
Key Point

__exit__ receives exception info. If it returns True, the exception is suppressed. If it returns None/False, the exception propagates.

Pro Tip

Use from contextlib import contextmanager and @contextmanager to create a context manager from a generator function — much simpler than writing a class.
followups: What dunder methods does a context manager implement?
How do you create a context manager with contextlib?
What is the difference between __enter__ and __exit__?

questions.q: How do you handle CSV files in Python?
questions.short: Use the csv module to read and write CSV files. csv.reader() returns rows as lists. csv.DictReader() returns rows as dicts keyed by column headers.
questions.detailed:
• import csv.
  • Read with DictReader: with open('data.csv') as f: reader = csv.DictReader(f); for row in reader: print(row['column']).
  • Write with DictWriter: writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['a','b']); writer.writeheader(); writer.writerow({'a':1,'b':2}).
  • Handle different delimiters: csv.reader(f, delimiter='\t') for tab-separated.
Key Point

Always open CSV files with newline='': open('file.csv', newline=''). Without it, the csv module may mishandle line endings on Windows.

Pro Tip

For data analysis, pandas.read_csv() is much more powerful than the csv module — it handles encoding, missing values, type inference, and more automatically.
followups: What is the difference between csv.reader and csv.DictReader?
How do you write to a CSV file?
Why should you use newline='' when opening CSV files?

questions.q: Differentiate between text mode and binary mode in file handling.
questions.short: Text mode (default) reads/writes strings and handles newline translation. Binary mode ('b') reads/writes raw bytes with no translation.
questions.detailed:
• Text mode ('r', 'w', 'a'): Returns str. Newlines are translated (\r\n on Windows becomes \n).
  • Binary mode ('rb', 'wb', 'ab'): Returns bytes. No newline translation. Exact byte-for-byte reading/writing.
  • Use binary mode for: images, audio, video, PDFs, pickled objects, any non-text data.
  • Use text mode for: plain text files, CSV, JSON, logs.
Key Point

When working with non-text files (images, PDFs, zip files), always use binary mode. Text mode's newline translation would corrupt binary data.

Common Trap

Reading a binary file in text mode (default) can raise UnicodeDecodeError because binary data is not valid UTF-8. Always use 'rb' for binary files.
followups: When would you use binary mode?
What is the difference between bytes and str in Python?
How do you read an image file in Python?

questions.q: What is exception handling in Python and why is it important?
questions.short: Exception handling lets you gracefully manage runtime errors instead of letting them crash the program. Use try-except to catch and handle errors.
questions.detailed:
• Without exception handling, any runtime error stops the program immediately.
  • With try-except, you catch errors, handle them, and continue or fail gracefully.
  • Helps distinguish expected errors (file not found) from bugs (wrong logic).
  • Makes code more robust for production use.
Key Point

Exception handling is for EXPECTED error conditions (file missing, network timeout, invalid user input). It is NOT for catching bugs — fix bugs, do not hide them with try-except.

Common Trap

Catching all exceptions with a bare except: or except Exception: hides bugs and makes debugging very hard. Always catch the most specific exception type you expect.
followups: What is the difference between an error and an exception?
When should you NOT use try-except?
What is the Python exception hierarchy?

questions.q: Classify the different types of errors in Python.
questions.short: Python has three error types: SyntaxError (invalid code), RuntimeError (errors during execution), and LogicalError (wrong output, no exception raised).
questions.detailed:
• SyntaxError: Invalid Python syntax. Caught before execution. if x without colon.
  • IndentationError: Subclass of SyntaxError. Wrong indentation.
  • RuntimeError (Exceptions): Raised during execution.
    • NameError: Variable not defined.
    • TypeError: Wrong type for operation.
    • ValueError: Right type, wrong value. int("abc").
    • KeyError: Dict key not found.
    • IndexError: List index out of range.
    • AttributeError: Object has no such attribute.
    • ZeroDivisionError: Division by zero.
    • FileNotFoundError: File does not exist.
  • LogicalError: Code runs without error but produces wrong results. Not catchable — requires debugging.
Key Point

SyntaxErrors are caught by the parser before execution. All other errors (exceptions) are raised at runtime. Logical errors are never raised — the program runs, just wrongly.
followups: What is the difference between SyntaxError and RuntimeError?
What is ValueError vs TypeError?
Can you catch a SyntaxError?

questions.q: Explain the try, except, else, and finally blocks.
questions.short: try runs code that might fail. except handles specific errors. else runs if no exception occurred. finally always runs regardless of whether an exception was raised.
questions.detailed:
• try:: Code that might raise an exception.
  • except SomeError as e:: Runs if SomeError is raised. as e captures the exception object.
  • except (TypeA, TypeB):: Catch multiple exception types.
  • else:: Runs only if NO exception was raised in try.
  • finally:: ALWAYS runs — whether an exception occurred or not. Used for cleanup.
Key Point

The else block is underused but powerful. Put code that should only run on success there, keeping the try block minimal.

Common Trap

The finally block runs even if there is a return inside try or except. Be careful with return in finally — it overrides the return value from try/except.
followups: When does the else block run?
Does finally run even if there is a return in try?
Can you have multiple except blocks?

questions.q: How do you raise and create custom exceptions in Python?
questions.short: Use raise to manually raise an exception. Create custom exceptions by subclassing Exception.
questions.detailed:
• raise ValueError("message"): Raise a specific exception.
  • raise: Re-raise the current exception inside an except block.
  • Custom exception:
    • class AppError(Exception):
    • def __init__(self, msg, code=0):
    • super().__init__(msg)
    • self.code = code
  • Raise it: raise AppError("Something failed", code=404).
Key Point

Custom exceptions make your API more expressive. Callers can catch your specific error type instead of a generic Exception.

Pro Tip

Create a base exception class for your application (class AppBaseError(Exception): pass) and have all your custom exceptions inherit from it. Callers can catch all your errors with except AppBaseError.
followups: How do you create a custom exception?
When would you raise an exception?
What is raise from?

questions.q: Which errors can be caught using try-except?
questions.short: All exceptions that inherit from Exception can be caught. BaseException subclasses (like SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt) should generally not be caught.
questions.detailed:
• All built-in exceptions inherit from BaseException.
  • Exception is the base for all non-system exceptions — catch this for general error handling.
  • SystemExit: Raised by sys.exit(). Do not catch unless you need cleanup.
  • KeyboardInterrupt: Raised by Ctrl+C. Catching it prevents user from stopping the program.
  • GeneratorExit: Raised when a generator is closed.
  • SyntaxErrors cannot be caught at runtime (they prevent execution).
Key Point

Only catch exceptions you can meaningfully handle. Catching Exception broadly is acceptable for top-level error logging, but always log the error rather than silently ignoring it.

Common Trap

Never use a bare except: without specifying an exception type. It catches SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt, preventing the user from stopping the program.
followups: What is the difference between Exception and BaseException?
What does KeyboardInterrupt inherit from?
Can you catch SystemExit?

questions.q: Differentiate between raise and raise from in Python.
questions.short: raise reraises the current exception. raise X from Y chains exceptions — Y becomes the cause of X, preserving context for debugging.
questions.detailed:
• raise: Inside an except block, re-raises the caught exception unchanged.
  • raise NewError("msg") from original: Creates an exception chain. The original is stored as __cause__.
  • raise NewError("msg") from None: Suppresses the context — hides the original exception.
  • Exception chaining helps trace the root cause through multiple layers of abstraction.
Key Point

Use raise X from Y when you catch a low-level exception and want to raise a higher-level one without losing the original context. The traceback shows both.

Common Trap

raise NewError("msg") inside an except block implicitly chains the exceptions (stored in __context__). Use from None to explicitly suppress this if the original error is an implementation detail.
followups: What is __cause__ vs __context__?
What is implicit exception chaining?
When would you use raise from None?

questions.q: What is the Python exception hierarchy?
questions.short: All exceptions inherit from BaseException. Exception is the base for user-catchable exceptions. Common subclasses: ValueError, TypeError, KeyError, IndexError, AttributeError.
questions.detailed:
• BaseException
    • SystemExit
    • KeyboardInterrupt
    • GeneratorExit
    • Exception
      • ArithmeticError (ZeroDivisionError, OverflowError)
      • LookupError (KeyError, IndexError)
      • ValueError
      • TypeError
      • NameError (UnboundLocalError)
      • AttributeError
      • IOError (FileNotFoundError, PermissionError)
      • RuntimeError
      • StopIteration
Key Point

Catch the most specific exception you can. Catching LookupError catches both KeyError and IndexError. Catching Exception catches almost everything.

Pro Tip

You can see all built-in exceptions with: print(dir(builtins)) filtered for Error/Exception, or just use the Python docs.
followups: What is the difference between LookupError and ValueError?
What exceptions should you never catch?
How do you create a custom exception hierarchy?

questions.q: How do you handle multiple exceptions in a single except block?
questions.short: Handle multiple exceptions by listing them in a tuple: except (TypeError, ValueError). Or use separate except blocks for different handling logic.
questions.detailed:
• Tuple syntax (same handling): except (TypeError, ValueError) as e:.
  • Multiple except blocks (different handling):
    • except ValueError: handle_val()
    • except TypeError: handle_type()
    • except Exception as e: log(e)
  • Order matters: more specific exceptions must come before more general ones.
  • except Exception catches almost all exceptions — use last as fallback.
Key Point

Put the most specific exception types first. If you put except Exception before except ValueError, the ValueError block is never reached.

Common Trap

Each except clause is checked in order. Python stops at the first matching one. Putting broad exceptions before narrow ones is a silent bug.
followups: What is the order of except blocks?
Can you have an except block without specifying an exception type?
What is the difference between except E1, E2 and except (E1, E2)?

questions.q: What is exception chaining in Python?
questions.short: Exception chaining links exceptions so you can see the chain of errors. Implicit chaining happens automatically; explicit chaining uses raise X from Y.
questions.detailed:
• Implicit chaining: When an exception is raised inside an except block, Python automatically sets the original exception as __context__.
  • Explicit chaining: raise NewError("msg") from original_error sets __cause__. The traceback says "The above exception was the direct cause of..."
  • Suppress chaining: raise NewError() from None hides the original exception.
Key Point

Exception chaining makes debugging easier — you can trace the root cause through multiple layers. Always use raise X from Y when wrapping low-level exceptions in higher-level ones.

Pro Tip

When building libraries, catch low-level exceptions (like OSError) and raise your own custom exceptions using from e so callers see a clean API but the original cause is preserved.
followups: What is __cause__ vs __context__?
What does raise from None do?
How do you inspect the cause of an exception?

questions.q: Differentiate between a module and a package in Python.
questions.short: A module is a single .py file containing Python code. A package is a directory containing multiple modules and an __init__.py file.
questions.detailed:
• Module: A .py file. Import with import math or from math import sqrt.
  • Package: A directory with an __init__.py file. Can contain modules and sub-packages.
  • A package is a module that contains other modules.
  • Example: numpy is a package. numpy.random is a sub-package. numpy.random.seed is a function in that module.
Key Point

Every Python file is a module. The directory structure with __init__.py is what makes a group of modules into a package.

Pro Tip

In Python 3.3+, implicit namespace packages allow directories without __init__.py to be treated as packages. But explicit __init__.py is still best practice.
followups: What is __init__.py?
What is a namespace package?
How do you import from a package?

questions.q: How does the import statement work in Python?
questions.short: import searches for the module in sys.path, compiles it to bytecode if needed, executes it, and makes its namespace available.
questions.detailed:
• import math: Imports the math module. Access with math.sqrt.
  • from math import sqrt: Imports sqrt directly into the current namespace.
  • from math import sqrt as sq: Import with alias.
  • import numpy as np: Import module with alias.
  • Python checks sys.path in order: current dir, PYTHONPATH, standard library, site-packages.
Key Point

Modules are executed once and cached in sys.modules. Subsequent imports reuse the cached version — they do NOT re-execute the module code.

Common Trap

Circular imports (module A imports B, B imports A) can cause ImportError or partial imports. Restructure your code or use late imports inside functions to avoid this.
followups: What is sys.path?
What is sys.modules?
What are circular imports?

questions.q: What does if __name__ == '__main__' mean?
questions.short: __name__ is a special variable. When a file is run directly, __name__ is "__main__". When imported as a module, __name__ is the module name.
questions.detailed:
• Python sets __name__ = "__main__" for the file that is executed directly.
  • For imported files, __name__ is set to the module name (file name without .py).
  • if __name__ == "__main__": guards code that should only run when the script is executed directly, not when imported.
Common use cases:
  • Running tests or demos from a module file.
  • Providing a CLI entry point in a module that is also importable as a library.
  • Preventing side effects when the module is imported.
Key Point

This idiom is essential for writing reusable modules. Always put executable code (not just definitions) inside if __name__ == "__main__":.

Common Trap

If you have code at the module level outside if __name__ == "__main__":, it runs every time the module is imported — even if the importer does not need it.
followups: Why is if __name__ == '__main__' important?
What is __name__ when a module is imported?
How do you structure a Python script that is also a library?

questions.q: What is the role of __init__.py in a package?
questions.short: __init__.py marks a directory as a Python package. It is executed when the package is imported and can define what the package exports.
questions.detailed:
• An empty __init__.py is enough to make a directory a package.
  • Code inside __init__.py runs when the package is first imported.
  • Use it to expose a clean public API: import submodule names so callers can use package.function instead of package.module.function.
  • __all__ in __init__.py controls what from package import * exports.
Key Point

In modern Python (3.3+), packages can exist without __init__.py (namespace packages), but adding an empty one is still good practice for clarity.

Pro Tip

Keep __init__.py lean. Importing too much in it slows down all imports of the package. Import only what needs to be in the top-level namespace.
followups: What happens if __init__.py is missing?
What is __all__?
What is a namespace package?

questions.q: Differentiate between absolute and relative imports.
questions.short: Absolute imports use the full path from the project root. Relative imports use dots to indicate position relative to the current module.
questions.detailed:
• Absolute: from mypackage.utils import helper. Always works regardless of where you are.
  • Relative: from . import utils (same package), from .. import config (parent package).
  • Relative imports only work inside packages — not in scripts run directly.
  • PEP 8 recommends absolute imports over relative imports for clarity.
Key Point

Absolute imports are clearer and work from anywhere. Relative imports are useful when you want to keep a package self-contained and portable without hard-coding package names.

Common Trap

You cannot use relative imports in a script that is run directly (python script.py). They only work when the file is imported as part of a package.
followups: When can you use relative imports?
What does from . import mean?
Why does PEP 8 prefer absolute imports?

questions.q: What are some commonly used built-in Python modules?
questions.short: Commonly used built-in modules: os, sys, math, random, datetime, collections, itertools, functools, json, re, pathlib, csv.
questions.detailed:
• os: File system operations, environment variables, process management.
  • sys: Python runtime info, sys.path, sys.argv, sys.exit().
  • math: Mathematical functions — sqrt, floor, ceil, log.
  • random: Random number generation.
  • datetime: Date and time handling.
  • collections: Counter, defaultdict, OrderedDict, deque.
  • itertools: Efficient iterators — chain, product, combinations.
  • functools: lru_cache, partial, reduce.
  • json: JSON serialisation and parsing.
  • re: Regular expressions.
  • pathlib: Modern object-oriented file path handling.
Key Point

The Python standard library is vast. Knowing the right module saves you from writing code that already exists. Check the docs before writing utility functions.

Pro Tip

For data science interviews, also know: csv, io, copy, abc, typing, dataclasses.
followups: What does the os module provide?
What is the collections module?
What is itertools used for?

questions.q: What is pip and how do you use it?
questions.short: pip is Python's package manager. Use pip install package to install, pip uninstall to remove, pip list to see installed packages, pip freeze to export requirements.
questions.detailed:
• pip install numpy: Install a package.
  • pip install numpy==1.24.0: Install a specific version.
  • pip install -r requirements.txt: Install all packages from a file.
  • pip uninstall numpy: Remove a package.
  • pip list: Show all installed packages.
  • pip freeze > requirements.txt: Export installed packages to a file.
  • pip show numpy: Show details about a package.
Key Point

Always use pip inside a virtual environment to avoid polluting the global Python installation.

Common Trap

Running pip install without a virtual environment installs packages globally, which can cause version conflicts between projects. Always activate a venv first.
followups: What is a virtual environment?
What is requirements.txt?
What is the difference between pip and conda?

questions.q: What is a virtual environment and why is it used?
questions.short: A virtual environment is an isolated Python environment with its own packages, independent from other projects and the global Python installation.
questions.detailed:
• Create: python -m venv myenv.
  • Activate (Windows): myenv\Scripts\activate.
  • Activate (macOS/Linux): source myenv/bin/activate.
  • Deactivate: deactivate.
  • Each project gets its own packages and versions — no conflicts between projects.
Key Point

Always create a virtual environment for each project. This prevents "dependency hell" where project A needs numpy 1.20 and project B needs numpy 2.0.

Pro Tip

Modern tools like poetry, pipenv, and uv manage virtual environments and dependencies automatically. For basic use, the built-in venv module is enough.
followups: What is the difference between venv and virtualenv?
What is pipenv?
How do you share a project's dependencies with others?

questions.q: What is requirements.txt and why is it important?
questions.short: requirements.txt lists all Python packages (with versions) needed to run a project. It lets others reproduce your exact environment with pip install -r requirements.txt.
questions.detailed:
• Generate: pip freeze > requirements.txt.
  • Install from: pip install -r requirements.txt.
  • Format: one package per line — numpy==1.24.0.
  • Use pip freeze to capture exact current versions.
  • Use pip list --format=freeze for the same effect.
Key Point

requirements.txt ensures reproducibility. Without it, running your code on another machine might fail because different package versions are installed.

Pro Tip

For production projects, pin exact versions (numpy==1.24.0) to ensure reproducibility. For libraries you publish, use ranges (numpy>=1.20) to avoid over-constraining users.
followups: What is the difference between requirements.txt and setup.py?
How do you keep requirements.txt up to date?
What is a lock file?

questions.q: What are generators in Python and how do they work?
questions.short: A generator is a function that uses yield to produce values one at a time. It is lazy — values are generated on demand, saving memory.
questions.detailed:
• A function with yield becomes a generator function.
  • Calling it returns a generator object — the code does not run yet.
  • next() on the generator runs it until the next yield, pauses, and returns the yielded value.
  • When the function ends (or hits return), it raises StopIteration.
  • Example: def count_up(n): while n > 0: yield n; n -= 1.
Key Point

Generators are memory-efficient because they produce values on demand instead of building the whole sequence. range() is a lazy generator — range(1000000) uses almost no memory.

Common Trap

Generators are exhausted after one pass. Once all values are yielded, the generator is empty. Iterating it again gives nothing. Create a new generator if you need to iterate again.
followups: What is the difference between a generator and a list?
What is yield?
What is a generator expression?

questions.q: Differentiate between return and yield.
questions.short: return exits a function and sends back a single value. yield pauses execution, sends a value to the caller, and resumes from where it left off when next() is called again.
questions.detailed:
• return: Exits the function permanently. All local state is lost.
  • yield: Pauses the function, saves all local state, and sends a value to the caller.
  • A function with yield is a generator — it maintains state between calls.
  • A function can have both yield and return: return stops the generator and raises StopIteration.
Key Point

yield turns a function into a stateful, resumable coroutine-like object. The function body is not re-executed from scratch on each call — it resumes from the last yield.

Common Trap

A function with yield always returns a generator object when called — the body does not execute until you call next() on it. This trips up many beginners.
followups: What is yield from?
What is a generator?
Can a function have both yield and return?

questions.q: What are decorators in Python? How do you create one?
questions.short: A decorator is a function that wraps another function to add behaviour before/after it. Use @decorator_name syntax as shorthand.
questions.detailed:
• A decorator takes a function, wraps it, and returns the wrapper.
  • @decorator above a function is syntactic sugar for fn = decorator(fn).
  • Common uses: logging, timing, authentication, caching, input validation.
Basic decorator pattern:
  • def timer(func):
  • import functools
  • @functools.wraps(func)
  • def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
  • # before
  • result = func(*args, **kwargs)
  • # after
  • return result
  • return wrapper
Key Point

Always use @functools.wraps(func) inside your decorator. Without it, the wrapped function loses its __name__, __doc__, and other metadata.

Common Trap

Forgetting @functools.wraps(func) means all decorated functions have the name "wrapper". This breaks logging, debugging, and any code that uses fn.__name__.
followups: What is functools.wraps?
How do you write a decorator with arguments?
What are class-based decorators?

questions.q: What is the GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) in Python?
questions.short: The GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) is a mutex in CPython that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, even on multi-core systems.
questions.detailed:
• CPython uses reference counting for memory management. The GIL prevents race conditions on reference counts.
  • One thread runs at a time in the Python interpreter — true parallel execution of Python code is not possible with threads.
  • The GIL is released during I/O operations (file reads, network calls) — so threading is useful for I/O-bound tasks.
  • For CPU-bound parallel work, use multiprocessing (separate processes, each with their own GIL).
Key Point

The GIL does NOT prevent concurrency for I/O-bound work. Use threading for I/O-bound tasks (web requests, file reads). Use multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks (computation, data processing).

Common Trap

Many developers assume multithreading in Python gives CPU parallelism. It does not — the GIL prevents that. Two threads competing for the CPU actually slow things down due to GIL contention. Use multiprocessing instead.
followups: How do you achieve parallelism in Python?
What is the difference between threading and multiprocessing?
Does asyncio help with the GIL?

questions.q: How does memory management work in Python?
questions.short: Python manages memory automatically using reference counting and a cyclic garbage collector. When an object's reference count drops to zero, it is immediately deallocated.
questions.detailed:
• Reference counting: Every object tracks how many references point to it. When count = 0, memory is freed immediately.
  • Garbage collector: Handles reference cycles (A refers to B, B refers to A) that reference counting cannot resolve. Runs periodically.
  • Memory allocator: Python has its own allocator on top of the OS allocator for efficiency.
  • Access reference count: import sys; sys.getrefcount(obj).
Key Point

Python's memory management is largely automatic. You rarely need to think about it. The main thing to know: avoid keeping large objects in memory longer than needed (del, or let them go out of scope).

Pro Tip

For performance-critical code, use memory-efficient data structures (array, numpy) instead of lists of Python objects. Each Python object has significant memory overhead.
followups: What is reference counting?
What is a reference cycle?
What is the gc module?

questions.q: What is garbage collection in Python?
questions.short: Garbage collection in Python handles objects that reference counting cannot free — specifically reference cycles. The gc module provides control over it.
questions.detailed:
• Reference cycles: A references B, B references A. Neither reaches zero reference count through normal counting.
  • Python's cyclic GC detects and collects these cycles.
  • Runs automatically in generations (gen 0 most frequent, gen 2 least).
  • import gc; gc.collect(): Manually trigger garbage collection.
  • gc.disable() and gc.enable(): Control GC.
  • gc.get_count(): Get object counts per generation.
Key Point

In CPython, most objects are freed immediately by reference counting. The cyclic GC is only needed for objects involved in reference cycles, which are relatively rare.

Pro Tip

In performance-critical code, you can disable the GC temporarily with gc.disable() if you know you have no reference cycles. Re-enable it after with gc.enable().
followups: What is a reference cycle?
What is generational garbage collection?
When would you call gc.collect() manually?

questions.q: What is reference counting in Python?
questions.short: Reference counting tracks how many names/containers point to each object. When the count reaches zero, the memory is immediately freed.
questions.detailed:
• Every Python object has a ob_refcnt field.
  • Count increases: when assigned to a variable, passed to a function, added to a list.
  • Count decreases: when a variable goes out of scope, del is called, or a container is modified.
  • sys.getrefcount(obj) returns the current count. Note: calling this function itself adds 1 temporarily.
Key Point

Reference counting enables immediate memory freeing — as soon as the last reference is dropped, memory is freed. This is more predictable than a GC that runs periodically.

Common Trap

sys.getrefcount(obj) always returns at least 1 higher than you expect because passing obj to getrefcount() itself creates a temporary reference.
followups: What is the difference between reference counting and garbage collection?
What is sys.getrefcount()?
What happens when an object's reference count hits zero?

questions.q: What are type hints in Python and why are they useful?
questions.short: Type hints annotate variables and functions with expected types. They are not enforced at runtime but enable static type checkers (mypy, pyright) and improve IDE support.
questions.detailed:
• Function: def greet(name: str) -> str:.
  • Variable: count: int = 0.
  • Optional: from typing import Optional; def fn(x: Optional[int] = None):.
  • Collections: list[int], dict[str, int], tuple[int, str] (Python 3.9+).
  • Union: int | str (Python 3.10+) or Union[int, str].
Key Point

Type hints are completely optional and not enforced by Python at runtime. They are checked by external tools. Adding them improves code quality, IDE autocompletion, and catches bugs before running.

Pro Tip

Use mypy to statically check your type hints: pip install mypy; mypy your_script.py. It finds type errors without running the code.
followups: What is mypy?
Are type hints enforced at runtime?
What is the difference between Optional and Union?

questions.q: What is Big O notation and why does it matter?
questions.short: Big O notation describes how an algorithm's time or space requirements grow as input size n increases. It characterises the worst-case growth rate.
questions.detailed:
• O(1): Constant time. Does not depend on input size. Dict lookup, list append.
  • O(log n): Logarithmic. Binary search.
  • O(n): Linear. Scanning a list, linear search.
  • O(n log n): Linearithmic. Efficient sorting (Timsort, merge sort).
  • O(n²): Quadratic. Nested loops over n elements.
  • O(2^n): Exponential. Recursive Fibonacci without memoisation.
Key Point

Big O ignores constants and lower-order terms. O(2n) is simplified to O(n). It describes the rate of growth, not the exact time.

Pro Tip

Always mention time and space complexity in coding interviews. Use the notation: "This runs in O(n) time and O(1) space." It signals you think about performance, not just correctness.
followups: What is the difference between O(n) and O(n²)?
What is space complexity?
What is the best, worst, and average case?

questions.q: Explain time complexity and space complexity.
questions.short: Time complexity measures how runtime grows with input size. Space complexity measures how memory usage grows with input size.
questions.detailed:
• Time complexity: Counts the number of operations as a function of input size n.
  • Space complexity: Counts the memory used as a function of input size n.
  • Example: iterating a list is O(n) time, O(1) space.
  • Example: creating a copy of a list is O(n) time, O(n) space.
  • Example: recursive Fibonacci is O(2^n) time, O(n) space (call stack).
Key Point

In interviews, analyse both time AND space. An algorithm can be fast but use too much memory, or be memory-efficient but slow. Interviewers often ask: "Can you optimise the space complexity?"

Common Trap

Recursion uses call stack space. A recursive function with depth n uses O(n) space even if it looks like it uses no data structures. This is a common oversight.
followups: What is auxiliary space?
What is the space complexity of recursion?
How do you optimise space complexity?

questions.q: Differentiate between multithreading and multiprocessing in Python.
questions.short: Multithreading runs multiple threads in the same process. Multiprocessing runs multiple processes with separate memory spaces. Use threads for I/O-bound tasks, processes for CPU-bound tasks.
questions.detailed:
• Multithreading: Threads share memory. Lightweight. Good for I/O-bound tasks (network, file I/O). Limited by GIL for CPU work.
  • Multiprocessing: Separate processes, separate memory. No GIL limitation. Good for CPU-bound tasks. Higher overhead.
  • threading module for threads. multiprocessing module for processes.
  • concurrent.futures: High-level API for both — ThreadPoolExecutor and ProcessPoolExecutor.
Key Point

The GIL makes Python threads ineffective for CPU-bound parallelism. For CPU-intensive work, always use multiprocessing or a C extension that releases the GIL.

Pro Tip

For most practical concurrency needs, use concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor for I/O tasks and concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU tasks. They share a clean, consistent API.
followups: What is the GIL?
What is asyncio?
When would you use multiprocessing over threading?

questions.q: Differentiate between concurrency and parallelism.
questions.short: Concurrency means handling multiple tasks that can overlap in time. Parallelism means executing multiple tasks simultaneously on multiple CPU cores.
questions.detailed:
• Concurrency: Multiple tasks make progress by interleaving. Not necessarily running at the same time. One CPU, many tasks.
  • Parallelism: Multiple tasks run simultaneously on multiple cores. True parallel execution.
  • Python concurrency options:
    • threading: Concurrent I/O (limited CPU parallelism due to GIL).
    • asyncio: Concurrent I/O with cooperative multitasking (single thread).
    • multiprocessing: True CPU parallelism.
Key Point

Concurrency is about DEALING WITH multiple things at once (structure). Parallelism is about DOING multiple things at once (execution). You can have concurrency without parallelism.

Pro Tip

"Concurrency is not parallelism" — Rob Pike. Python achieves concurrency with threads and asyncio, but only achieves true CPU parallelism with multiprocessing.
followups: What is asyncio?
What is the event loop?
How does asyncio differ from threading?
